# Appendix

## Pandemic Risk Perceptions Over Time {#sec-perception-appendix}

```{r}
#| label: load-perception-data
#| include: false

# Load perception by wave data
perceptions_by_wave <- read_csv("../out/tables/perceptions_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(!is.na(pct_very_concerned)) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

perception_gaps <- read_csv("../out/tables/perception_gaps_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(!is.na(concern_gap)) %>%
  mutate(
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

# Weekly cases data (wave 1: April 2020, ~200k weekly cases at the time)
weekly_cases <- tibble(
  wave = 1:6,
  cases_thousands = c(200, 693, 772, 2351, 2192, 545),
  wave_label = factor(c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"),
                      levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
)

# Summary stats
max_gap_wave <- perception_gaps %>% slice_max(concern_gap, n = 1)
min_gap_wave <- perception_gaps %>% slice_min(concern_gap, n = 1)
```

The main text documents substantial partisan differences in pandemic risk perception, with Democrats more likely than Republicans to report being "very concerned" about COVID-19. @fig-perception shows the overall distribution of concern by party, pooled across all waves. This section then examines how these perception gaps evolved over the course of the pandemic, paralleling our analysis of behavioral gaps over time.

```{r}
#| label: fig-perception
#| fig-cap: "Partisan Differences in Pandemic Risk Perception. Bars show the percentage of respondents reporting being 'very concerned' about COVID-19, by political party. Values are weighted means using raked survey weights, pooled across all survey waves."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 5

library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)

# Load paper statistics for pooled perception values
stats_path <- here::here("out", "tables", "paper_statistics.rds")
paper_stats <- readRDS(stats_path)
invisible(list2env(paper_stats, envir = environment()))

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Republican" = "#c50000", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Democrat" = "#326ba4")

# Create data for perception plot
perception_data <- tibble(
  party = c("Democrat", "Independent", "Republican"),
  value = c(concern_dem, concern_ind, concern_rep)
) %>%
  mutate(party = factor(party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")))

# Create bar plot
ggplot(perception_data, aes(x = party, y = value, fill = party)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", value)),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Very Concerned (%)") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 11)
  )
```

@fig-perception-levels displays the percentage of each partisan group reporting being "very concerned" about COVID-19 across survey waves. @fig-perception-gap shows the Democrat-Republican gap in this measure over time, with weekly COVID-19 cases shown in the background for context.

```{r}
#| label: fig-perception-levels
#| fig-cap: "Pandemic Risk Perception by Party Across Survey Waves. Lines show the percentage of respondents reporting being 'very concerned' about COVID-19. Gray background bars indicate weekly COVID-19 cases (in thousands)."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 8

library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Republican" = "#c50000", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Democrat" = "#326ba4")

# Scaling for dual axis - ensure bars fit within y-axis
max_cases <- max(weekly_cases$cases_thousands)
max_concern <- 80  # Scale cases to max out at 80% so they don't overwhelm the perception lines
concern_scale <- max_concern / max_cases

ggplot() +
  # Background: cases
  geom_col(data = weekly_cases, aes(x = wave_label, y = cases_thousands * concern_scale),
           fill = "gray80", alpha = 0.5, width = 0.7) +
  # Lines for each party
  geom_line(data = perceptions_by_wave,
            aes(x = wave_label, y = pct_very_concerned, color = political_party, group = political_party),
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(data = perceptions_by_wave,
             aes(x = wave_label, y = pct_very_concerned, color = political_party),
             size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Very Concerned (%)",
    limits = c(0, 85),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / concern_scale, name = "Weekly Cases (thousands)"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, color = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "gray50"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-perception-gap
#| fig-cap: "Partisan Gap in Pandemic Risk Perception Across Survey Waves. Points show the Democrat-Republican difference in the percentage reporting being 'very concerned' about COVID-19. Gray background bars indicate weekly COVID-19 cases (in thousands)."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 7

# Merge gaps with cases
gaps_for_plot <- perception_gaps %>%
  left_join(weekly_cases, by = c("wave", "wave_label"))

# Scaling
max_gap <- max(gaps_for_plot$concern_gap)
gap_scale <- max_gap / max(gaps_for_plot$cases_thousands) * 1.5

ggplot(gaps_for_plot, aes(x = wave_label)) +
  # Background: weekly cases
  geom_col(aes(y = cases_thousands * gap_scale),
           fill = "gray80", alpha = 0.5, width = 0.7) +
  # Foreground: perception gap as line + points
  geom_line(aes(y = concern_gap, group = 1), color = "#7570b3", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(y = concern_gap), color = "#7570b3", size = 4) +
  # Gap value labels
  geom_text(aes(y = concern_gap, label = sprintf("%.1f", concern_gap)),
            vjust = -1.2, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "D-R Gap (percentage points)",
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / gap_scale, name = "Weekly Cases (thousands)"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.2))
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "gray50")
  )
```

The perception gap was smallest at the beginning of the pandemic: just `r sprintf("%.1f", min_gap_wave$concern_gap)` percentage points in `r min_gap_wave$wave_label`. By `r max_gap_wave$wave_label`, the gap had grown to `r sprintf("%.1f", max_gap_wave$concern_gap)` percentage points. In April 2020, Republicans were nearly as likely as Democrats to report being "very concerned" about COVID-19; by June 2020, that gap had more than doubled, and it remained elevated throughout the remainder of the study period.

Critically, while incidence was relatively low in April 2020, *mortality rates were at their highest*---the virus was simply more deadly in the early months before treatments improved and clinical experience accumulated. This pattern is consistent with our broader finding that concrete, visible consequences reduce polarization. When the threat was most lethal, perceptions were least polarized. As case fatality rates declined over subsequent months---even as case counts rose---the perception gap widened. The combination of declining lethality and escalating politicization of the pandemic through debates over lockdowns, masks, and the credibility of public health guidance likely both contributed to this divergence.

All three partisan groups showed declining concern over time, particularly from the winter surge through spring 2021 as vaccines became widely available. However, this decline was steeper among Republicans and Independents than among Democrats, maintaining the perception gap even as overall concern fell.

### Perceptions vs. Local Pandemic Conditions {#sec-perception-vs-local}

The previous figures show perceptions against *national* case counts. But Republicans and Democrats live in different places and thus experienced different local pandemic conditions. @fig-perception-local shows each party's stated concern alongside the average local incidence rate they actually experienced, given where respondents in each party lived. This allows us to assess whether perceptions tracked the objective conditions each group faced.

```{r}
#| label: fig-perception-local
#| fig-cap: "Perceptions vs. Local Pandemic Conditions by Party. Each panel shows one party's stated concern (line) against the average local incidence rate experienced by that party's respondents (bars). Local incidence is calculated as the weighted average of county-level weekly incidence rates among respondents of each party."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10

library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Load perceptions with local conditions
perceptions_local <- read_csv("../out/tables/perceptions_with_local_conditions.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Democrat")),
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Republican" = "#c50000", "Democrat" = "#326ba4")

# Use common scale for both panels (based on max across both parties)
max_inc_overall <- max(perceptions_local$mean_local_incidence, na.rm = TRUE)
max_concern <- 80
inc_scale <- max_concern / max_inc_overall

# Create a plot for each party
make_party_plot <- function(party_name, party_color, show_y_left = TRUE, show_y_right = FALSE) {
  party_data <- perceptions_local %>% filter(political_party == party_name)

  p <- ggplot(party_data, aes(x = wave_label)) +
    # Background: local incidence
    geom_col(aes(y = mean_local_incidence * inc_scale),
             fill = "gray70", alpha = 0.6, width = 0.7) +
    # Line: stated concern
    geom_line(aes(y = pct_very_concerned, group = 1),
              color = party_color, linewidth = 1.3) +
    geom_point(aes(y = pct_very_concerned),
               color = party_color, size = 3.5) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      name = if(show_y_left) "Very Concerned (%)" else NULL,
      limits = c(0, 85),
      sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / inc_scale,
                          name = if(show_y_right) "Local Incidence (per 100k)" else NULL),
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
    ) +
    labs(x = NULL, title = party_name) +
    theme_cowplot() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = party_color, size = 14),
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "gray50", size = 9)
    )

  if (!show_y_left) p <- p + theme(axis.text.y.left = element_blank())
  if (!show_y_right) p <- p + theme(axis.text.y.right = element_blank())

  p
}

p_rep <- make_party_plot("Republican", "#c50000", show_y_left = TRUE, show_y_right = FALSE)
p_dem <- make_party_plot("Democrat", "#326ba4", show_y_left = FALSE, show_y_right = TRUE)

p_rep + p_dem
```

The figure reveals that Republicans and Democrats experienced similar local incidence trajectories---both groups saw the winter 2020--21 surge---yet their perceptions diverged over time. Notably, incidence was relatively low in April 2020 when the perception gap was smallest, complicating any simple story about incidence driving perceptions.

@fig-perception-local-mortality shows the same comparison using local mortality rates rather than incidence. Mortality is a more concrete and visible indicator of pandemic severity---deaths are reported in local news, experienced through social networks, and marked by obituaries---making it a stronger test of whether perceptions tracked objective conditions.

```{r}
#| label: fig-perception-local-mortality
#| fig-cap: "Perceptions vs. Local Mortality by Party. Each panel shows one party's stated concern (line) against the average local mortality rate experienced by that party's respondents (bars). Local mortality is calculated as the weighted average of county-level weekly mortality rates (per 100,000) among respondents of each party."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10

library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Load perceptions with local conditions (already loaded above, but reload for safety)
perceptions_local_mort <- read_csv("../out/tables/perceptions_with_local_conditions.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Democrat")),
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Republican" = "#c50000", "Democrat" = "#326ba4")

# Use common scale for both panels (based on max across both parties)
max_mort_overall <- max(perceptions_local_mort$mean_local_mortality, na.rm = TRUE)
max_concern <- 80
mort_scale <- max_concern / max_mort_overall

# Create a plot for each party
make_party_mort_plot <- function(party_name, party_color, show_y_left = TRUE, show_y_right = FALSE) {
  party_data <- perceptions_local_mort %>% filter(political_party == party_name)

  p <- ggplot(party_data, aes(x = wave_label)) +
    # Background: local mortality
    geom_col(aes(y = mean_local_mortality * mort_scale),
             fill = "gray70", alpha = 0.6, width = 0.7) +
    # Line: stated concern
    geom_line(aes(y = pct_very_concerned, group = 1),
              color = party_color, linewidth = 1.3) +
    geom_point(aes(y = pct_very_concerned),
               color = party_color, size = 3.5) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      name = if(show_y_left) "Very Concerned (%)" else NULL,
      limits = c(0, 85),
      sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / mort_scale,
                          name = if(show_y_right) "Local Mortality (per 100k)" else NULL),
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
    ) +
    labs(x = NULL, title = party_name) +
    theme_cowplot() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = party_color, size = 14),
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "gray50", size = 9)
    )

  if (!show_y_left) p <- p + theme(axis.text.y.left = element_blank())
  if (!show_y_right) p <- p + theme(axis.text.y.right = element_blank())

  p
}

p_rep_mort <- make_party_mort_plot("Republican", "#c50000", show_y_left = TRUE, show_y_right = FALSE)
p_dem_mort <- make_party_mort_plot("Democrat", "#326ba4", show_y_left = FALSE, show_y_right = TRUE)

p_rep_mort + p_dem_mort
```

The mortality figure reveals a striking pattern: local mortality rates were actually *highest* in April 2020---precisely when the perception gap was smallest. The virus was simply more deadly in the early months before treatments improved and clinical protocols developed. As mortality rates declined over subsequent months, the perception gap widened. This pattern supports our broader thesis that concrete, visible consequences reduce polarization: when the threat was most lethal, partisans were most aligned in their concern.

The winter 2020--21 surge presents a more complex picture. Mortality rose sharply, and Republicans did reduce their contacts in response---yet their stated concern continued to decline. This disconnect between mortality-driven *behavior* and diverging *perceptions* suggests that by this point, expressed concern had become entangled with partisan identity in ways that were resistant to objective conditions. The early-pandemic alignment, when partisanship had not yet fully politicized COVID-19, may represent a window before symbolic boundaries hardened. Once the pandemic became a site of partisan conflict, stated perceptions became partly performative---reflecting group allegiance rather than risk assessment---even as underlying behavior continued to respond to concrete threat.

### Health Behaviors by Concern Level {#sec-behavior-by-concern}

@fig-concern-behavior-appendix shows health behaviors by party within each level of stated concern. This complements @fig-concern-gaps in the main text by showing the absolute levels rather than the gaps.

```{r}
#| label: fig-concern-behavior-appendix
#| fig-cap: "Health Behaviors by Party Within Each Level of Stated Concern. Panel A shows mean daily contacts; Panel B shows mask usage. Sample sizes: Not at all concerned (n=1,127), Not very concerned (n=1,897), Somewhat concerned (n=5,894), Very concerned (n=9,168)."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10

library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)

# Load behaviors by concern level
behaviors_by_concern <- read_csv("../out/tables/behaviors_by_concern_level.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    concern_level = factor(concern_level,
                           levels = c("Not at all concerned", "Not very concerned",
                                      "Somewhat concerned", "Very concerned"))
  )

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Democrat" = "#326ba4", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Republican" = "#c50000")

# Panel A: Contacts by concern level
p_contacts <- ggplot(behaviors_by_concern, aes(x = concern_level, y = contacts, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", contacts)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), vjust = -0.5, size = 2.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("Not at all\nconcerned", "Not very\nconcerned",
                              "Somewhat\nconcerned", "Very\nconcerned")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean Daily Contacts", title = "A. Daily Contacts", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Panel B: Mask usage by concern level
p_masks <- ggplot(behaviors_by_concern, aes(x = concern_level, y = mask_usage, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.0f", mask_usage)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), vjust = -0.5, size = 2.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("Not at all\nconcerned", "Not very\nconcerned",
                              "Somewhat\nconcerned", "Very\nconcerned")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)", title = "B. Mask Usage", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Combine with shared legend
p_contacts + p_masks + plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

@fig-behavior-very-concerned shows how behavior among the "very concerned" subgroup evolved over time, alongside the changing share of each party reporting high concern. This analysis reveals that the meaning of "very concerned" changed over the pandemic as different types of partisans selected into this category.

```{r}
#| label: fig-behavior-very-concerned
#| fig-cap: "Behavior Among the 'Very Concerned' Over Time. Panel A shows the percentage reporting being 'very concerned'---declining for both parties but especially Republicans (55% to 26%). Panels B and C show behavior *only among those very concerned*. The contact gap (Panel B) was positive early on but collapsed during the winter surge and reversed by wave 5-6. The mask gap (Panel C) reversed earlier (wave 4) and persisted, suggesting politicization independent of concern level."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 12

library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)

# Load percent very concerned by wave
pct_vc <- read_csv("../out/tables/pct_very_concerned_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    wave_label = factor(wave, labels = c("W1\nApr'20", "W2\nJun'20", "W3\nSep'20",
                                          "W4\nDec'20", "W5\nFeb'21", "W6\nMay'21"))
  )

# Load behaviors among very concerned by wave
behaviors_vc <- read_csv("../out/tables/behaviors_among_very_concerned_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    wave_label = factor(wave, labels = c("W1\nApr'20", "W2\nJun'20", "W3\nSep'20",
                                          "W4\nDec'20", "W5\nFeb'21", "W6\nMay'21"))
  )

# Panel A: Percent very concerned by party over time
p_pct <- ggplot(pct_vc, aes(x = wave_label, y = pct_very_concerned, color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Democrat" = "#2166AC", "Republican" = "#B2182B")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "% 'Very Concerned'", title = "A. Share 'Very Concerned'", color = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7)) +
  ylim(20, 80)

# Panel B: Contacts among very concerned
p_contacts <- ggplot(behaviors_vc, aes(x = wave_label, y = contacts, color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Democrat" = "#2166AC", "Republican" = "#B2182B")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Contacts", title = "B. Contacts Among 'Very Concerned'", color = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7)) +
  ylim(3, 10)

# Panel C: Mask usage among very concerned
p_masks <- ggplot(behaviors_vc, aes(x = wave_label, y = mask_usage, color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Democrat" = "#2166AC", "Republican" = "#B2182B")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)", title = "C. Masks Among 'Very Concerned'", color = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7)) +
  ylim(60, 85)

p_pct + p_contacts + p_masks + plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Panel A shows that the share reporting "very concerned" declined for both parties, but especially for Republicans (55% to 26% vs. 67% to 49% for Democrats).
Panels B and C reveal how behavior among the very concerned evolved—and the patterns differ in revealing ways.

For contacts (Panel B), very concerned Republicans initially reported *more* contacts than very concerned Democrats (gap of +1.4 to +3.4 contacts in waves 1-3).
But this gap collapsed during the winter 2020-21 surge (wave 4-5) and actually reversed by waves 5-6, with concerned Democrats reporting slightly more contacts.
This timing aligns with the lifting of stay-in-place restrictions: among all respondents, the percentage reporting being under a stay-in-place order declined from approximately 95% in wave 1 to 70-81% by wave 5, with Republicans showing a larger decline than Democrats (@fig-sip-over-time in @sec-sip-appendix).
The contact pattern suggests mortality-driven convergence early on, followed by Democrats increasing contacts as restrictions lifted—perhaps reflecting a strategic shift toward relying on masks and vaccines rather than contact reduction.

For masks (Panel C), a similar reversal occurred but with different timing: very concerned Republicans wore masks at *higher* rates than very concerned Democrats in waves 1-3, but by wave 4 Democrats wore masks more.
Unlike contacts, this reversal did not correspond to the mortality surge or the lifting of restrictions—it persisted even as mortality declined in waves 5-6.
This pattern suggests that mask-wearing became increasingly tied to partisan identity over time, such that even "very concerned" Republicans reduced masking as the behavior became symbolically marked.

### Wave 1 Only: Gaps by Concern Level When Mortality Was Highest

The pooled analysis above combines data from all waves. To examine whether the relationship between concern and partisan gaps differed when objective threat was highest, @fig-concern-gaps-wave1 replicates this analysis for Wave 1 (April 2020) only---the period when mortality rates were at their peak.

```{r}
#| label: fig-concern-gaps-wave1
#| fig-cap: "Partisan Gaps in Health Behaviors by Concern Level, Wave 1 Only (April 2020). Panel A shows the Republican-Democrat gap in daily contacts; Panel B shows the Democrat-Republican gap in mask usage. Wave 1 represents the period of highest mortality rates, before the pandemic became heavily politicized. Binary concern grouping (Very Concerned vs. Less Concerned) used for statistical power. Unweighted means used since survey weights were designed for the full sample. Sample sizes shown on each bar (D = Democrats, R = Republicans)."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 10

library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)

# Load Wave 1 behaviors by concern level (binary: Very Concerned vs Less Concerned)
behaviors_wave1 <- read_csv("../out/tables/behaviors_by_concern_level_wave1.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    concern_level = factor(concern_level,
                           levels = c("Less Concerned", "Very Concerned"))
  )

# Calculate R-D gaps at each concern level
gaps_wave1 <- behaviors_wave1 %>%
  select(concern_level, political_party, contacts, mask_usage, n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = political_party, values_from = c(contacts, mask_usage, n)) %>%
  mutate(
    contacts_gap_rd = contacts_Republican - contacts_Democrat,
    mask_gap_dr = mask_usage_Democrat - mask_usage_Republican,
    n_label = paste0("D:", n_Democrat, " R:", n_Republican)
  )

# Panel A: Contact gap by concern level
p_contact_gap_w1 <- ggplot(gaps_wave1, aes(x = concern_level, y = contacts_gap_rd)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#7570b3", alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", contacts_gap_rd)),
            vjust = ifelse(gaps_wave1$contacts_gap_rd >= 0, -0.5, 1.5),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = n_label, y = 0), vjust = 1.5, size = 2.5, color = "gray40") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "R-D Contact Gap", title = "A. Contact Gap (Wave 1)") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10)
  )

# Panel B: Mask gap by concern level
p_mask_gap_w1 <- ggplot(gaps_wave1, aes(x = concern_level, y = mask_gap_dr)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1b9e77", alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", mask_gap_dr)),
            vjust = ifelse(gaps_wave1$mask_gap_dr >= 0, -0.5, 1.5),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = n_label, y = 0), vjust = 1.5, size = 2.5, color = "gray40") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "D-R Mask Gap (pp)", title = "B. Mask Gap (Wave 1)") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10)
  )

p_contact_gap_w1 + p_mask_gap_w1
```

Comparing Wave 1 to the pooled results reveals whether the early pandemic---before extensive politicization---showed different patterns. If concrete mortality threat reduces polarization, we might expect smaller or even reversed gaps in Wave 1 compared to later periods.

### The Politicization of Mask-Wearing: Exact Gap Values

@fig-behavior-very-concerned above shows how mask-wearing patterns among the "very concerned" reversed over time, alongside the changing composition of who reports being very concerned. @fig-mask-gap-very-concerned-over-time provides the exact D-R gap values for each wave.

```{r}
#| label: fig-mask-gap-very-concerned-over-time
#| fig-cap: "D-R Mask Gap Among 'Very Concerned' Respondents Over Time. Bars show the Democrat-Republican difference in mask usage among those reporting being 'very concerned' about COVID-19. Negative values (red) indicate Republicans wore masks more; positive values (blue) indicate Democrats wore masks more. The reversal from waves 1-3 to waves 4-6 is evident. See @fig-behavior-very-concerned for context on changing composition."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 7

library(cowplot)

# Load mask usage among very concerned by wave
mask_vc <- read_csv("../out/tables/mask_among_very_concerned_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    wave_label = factor(wave, labels = c("Wave 1\nApr '20", "Wave 2\nJun '20", "Wave 3\nSep '20",
                                          "Wave 4\nDec '20", "Wave 5\nFeb '21", "Wave 6\nMay '21"))
  )

# Calculate D-R gap by wave
gap_by_wave <- mask_vc %>%
  select(wave, wave_label, political_party, mask_usage) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = political_party, values_from = mask_usage) %>%
  mutate(
    gap_dr = Democrat - Republican,
    gap_color = ifelse(gap_dr >= 0, "D > R", "R > D")
  )

# D-R gap over time
ggplot(gap_by_wave, aes(x = wave_label, y = gap_dr, fill = gap_color)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%+.1f", gap_dr)),
            vjust = ifelse(gap_by_wave$gap_dr >= 0, -0.5, 1.5),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("D > R" = "#2166AC", "R > D" = "#B2182B")) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "D-R Mask Gap (pp)"
  ) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```

As discussed in the main text, two dynamics likely contribute to this reversal: (1) masks became increasingly politicized as partisan signals, and (2) the Republicans who continued to report being "very concerned" became an increasingly select and unusual subset as overall Republican concern declined.

## Stay-in-Place Orders Over Time {#sec-sip-appendix}

@fig-sip-over-time shows the percentage of respondents reporting being under a stay-in-place order across the study period. The BICS survey asked respondents whether they were currently under a stay-in-place order in waves 1-5, but dropped this question entirely in wave 6 (May 2021)---the only survey item discontinued during the study---reflecting the widespread lifting of restrictions by that time.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sip-over-time
#| fig-cap: "Percentage Reporting Being Under a Stay-in-Place Order Over Time. Panel A shows all respondents; Panel B shows only those reporting being 'very concerned' about COVID-19. Stay-in-place orders declined substantially over the study period. The question was dropped in wave 6 as most states had lifted formal restrictions by May 2021."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 10

library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)

# Load SIP data for all respondents
pct_sip_all <- read_csv("../out/tables/pct_under_sip_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    wave_label = factor(wave, levels = 1:6, labels = c("W1\nApr'20", "W2\nJun'20", "W3\nSep'20",
                                          "W4\nDec'20", "W5\nFeb'21", "W6\nMay'21"))
  )

# Load SIP data for very concerned only
pct_sip_vc <- read_csv("../out/tables/pct_under_sip_very_concerned_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    wave_label = factor(wave, levels = 1:6, labels = c("W1\nApr'20", "W2\nJun'20", "W3\nSep'20",
                                          "W4\nDec'20", "W5\nFeb'21", "W6\nMay'21"))
  )

# Panel A: All respondents
p_all <- ggplot(pct_sip_all, aes(x = wave_label, y = pct_under_sip, color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_discrete(drop = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Democrat" = "#2166AC", "Republican" = "#B2182B")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "% Under Stay-in-Place", title = "A. All Respondents", color = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8)) +
  annotate("text", x = 6, y = 75, label = "Question\ndropped", size = 2.5, hjust = 0.5, color = "gray40") +
  ylim(60, 100)

# Panel B: Very concerned only
p_vc <- ggplot(pct_sip_vc, aes(x = wave_label, y = pct_under_sip, color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_discrete(drop = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Democrat" = "#2166AC", "Republican" = "#B2182B")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "% Under Stay-in-Place", title = "B. 'Very Concerned' Only", color = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8)) +
  annotate("text", x = 6, y = 82, label = "Question\ndropped", size = 2.5, hjust = 0.5, color = "gray40") +
  ylim(70, 100)

p_all + p_vc + plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Among all respondents (Panel A), the percentage reporting being under a stay-in-place order declined from approximately 95% in wave 1 to 70-81% by wave 5, with Republicans showing a larger decline than Democrats (11 percentage point gap by wave 5). This overall pattern is more representative than the "very concerned" subset (Panel B), since the share of Republicans reporting being "very concerned" declined substantially over the study---from 55% in wave 1 to just 26% by wave 6---making the very concerned Republicans an increasingly selective group. The declining trend in stay-in-place orders provides context for the behavioral changes observed in the main text: as restrictions lifted, particularly between waves 4-6, Democrats increased their contacts while maintaining high mask usage---consistent with a strategic shift toward relying on masks and vaccines for protection as they resumed social activities.

## Interaction Models: Concern and Mortality as Moderators {#sec-interaction-models}

To formally test whether stated concern and objective mortality rates moderate partisan gaps in health behaviors, we estimate interaction models of the form:

$$\text{Behavior} = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\text{Republican}) + \beta_2(\text{Concern}) + \beta_3(\text{Mortality}) + \beta_4(\text{Rep} \times \text{Concern}) + \beta_5(\text{Rep} \times \text{Mortality}) + \text{FE}$$

In this specification, the partisan gap equals $\beta_1 + \beta_4(\text{Concern}) + \beta_5(\text{Mortality})$.
A negative $\beta_4$ indicates that higher concern *narrows* the partisan gap; a negative $\beta_5$ indicates that higher mortality narrows it.
Concern is measured as a binary indicator for reporting being "very concerned" about COVID-19; mortality is the standardized log of the previous week's county-level mortality rate.

We estimate two versions of this model to distinguish between national/temporal patterns and local variation:

1. **Wave FE model**: Includes wave fixed effects and controls for metropolitan status. This captures how the partisan gap varies with *national* pandemic severity (high-mortality waves vs. low-mortality waves).

2. **County FE model**: Includes county fixed effects. This asks: *within the same county*, when local mortality rises, does the partisan gap narrow? This isolates the effect of local conditions from all time-invariant county characteristics.

```{r}
#| label: load-interaction-results
#| include: false

# Load pre-computed statistics
stats_path <- "../out/tables/paper_statistics.rds"
if (file.exists(stats_path)) {
  paper_stats <- readRDS(stats_path)
  list2env(paper_stats, envir = environment())
}

# Load pre-computed interaction model results
interaction_all <- read_csv("../out/tables/interaction_model_results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(term %in% c("republican", "republican:very_concerned", "republican:log_mort_std")) %>%
  mutate(
    term_label = case_when(
      term == "republican" ~ "Baseline Gap",
      term == "republican:very_concerned" ~ "Rep × Concern",
      term == "republican:log_mort_std" ~ "Rep × Mortality"
    ),
    term_label = factor(term_label, levels = c("Baseline Gap",
                                                "Rep × Concern",
                                                "Rep × Mortality")),
    outcome = factor(outcome, levels = c("Contacts", "Masks")),
    model = factor(model, levels = c("Wave FE", "County FE"))
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-interaction-coefs
#| fig-cap: "Moderators of Partisan Gaps: Wave FE vs. County FE Models. Points show regression coefficients from interaction models predicting contacts (purple) and mask usage (green). Left panel uses wave fixed effects (national/temporal variation); right panel uses county fixed effects (local variation). 'Baseline Gap' is the Republican main effect. 'Rep × Concern' shows how being very concerned changes the gap. 'Rep × Mortality' shows how a 1 SD increase in local log mortality changes the gap. Negative values indicate the gap narrows. Error bars are 95% CIs."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10

library(ggplot2)
library(cowplot)

# Colors for the two outcomes
outcome_colors <- c("Contacts" = "#7570b3", "Masks" = "#1b9e77")

ggplot(interaction_all, aes(x = term_label, y = estimate, color = outcome)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
                      ymax = estimate + 1.96 * std.error),
                  position = position_dodge(width = 0.5), size = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = outcome_colors) +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Coefficient (effect on R-D gap)", color = "Outcome") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

@fig-interaction-coefs displays the results from both specifications.

**Wave FE Model (National/Temporal Patterns):**
For contacts, the baseline Republican-Democrat gap is `r sprintf("%.2f", contact_int_baseline)` contacts per day.
The concern interaction is small and not statistically significant ($\beta$ = `r sprintf("%.2f", contact_int_concern)`, p = `r sprintf("%.2f", contact_int_concern_p)`), confirming that stated concern does not moderate the contact gap.
The mortality interaction is negative ($\beta$ = `r sprintf("%.2f", contact_int_mort)`, p = `r sprintf("%.3f", contact_int_mort_p)`): during high-mortality waves, the partisan gap narrows.

For masks, the baseline gap is `r sprintf("%.1f", abs(mask_int_baseline))` percentage points.
The concern interaction is large and significant ($\beta$ = `r sprintf("%.1f", mask_int_concern)`, p < 0.001): being "very concerned" substantially narrows the mask gap.
The mortality interaction is smaller and in the opposite direction ($\beta$ = `r sprintf("%.1f", mask_int_mort)`).

**County FE Model (Local Variation):**
The county FE model asks whether the partisan gap narrows *within counties* when local mortality rises.
For contacts, the mortality interaction is `r sprintf("%.2f", contact_county_mort)` (p = `r sprintf("%.3f", contact_county_mort_p)`), indicating that local mortality conditions also moderate the partisan gap.
For masks, the local mortality interaction is `r sprintf("%.1f", mask_county_mort)` (p = `r sprintf("%.3f", mask_county_mort_p)`).

Both specifications support the same conclusion: **concern moderates the mask gap but not the contact gap; mortality moderates the contact gap but not the mask gap.**
This pattern holds whether we examine national trends (wave FE) or local variation (county FE), suggesting it reflects a genuine behavioral response to pandemic conditions rather than a compositional artifact.

## Adjusted Partisan Behaviors Over Time {#sec-adjusted-behaviors}

```{r}
#| label: load-adjusted-data
#| include: false

# Load adjusted behaviors data (from emmeans models)
adjusted_behaviors <- read_csv("../out/tables/adjusted_behaviors_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

adjusted_gaps <- read_csv("../out/tables/adjusted_gaps_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

# Separate by outcome and filter to just Republicans and Democrats
contacts_adjusted <- adjusted_behaviors %>%
  filter(outcome == "contacts", political_party != "Independent")
masks_adjusted <- adjusted_behaviors %>%
  filter(outcome == "masks", political_party != "Independent")
concern_adjusted <- adjusted_behaviors %>%
  filter(outcome == "concern", political_party != "Independent")

contacts_gaps <- adjusted_gaps %>% filter(outcome == "contacts")
masks_gaps <- adjusted_gaps %>% filter(outcome == "masks")
concern_gaps <- adjusted_gaps %>% filter(outcome == "concern")

# Summary stats for text
max_contact_gap_adj <- contacts_gaps %>% slice_max(gap_rep_dem, n = 1)
min_contact_gap_adj <- contacts_gaps %>% slice_min(gap_rep_dem, n = 1)
max_mask_gap_adj <- masks_gaps %>% slice_max(abs(gap_rep_dem), n = 1)
min_mask_gap_adj <- masks_gaps %>% slice_min(abs(gap_rep_dem), n = 1)
max_concern_gap_adj <- concern_gaps %>% slice_max(abs(gap_rep_dem), n = 1)
min_concern_gap_adj <- concern_gaps %>% slice_min(abs(gap_rep_dem), n = 1)
```

The main text presents descriptive partisan differences in health behaviors over time. To assess whether these patterns persist after accounting for demographic and contextual factors, we estimate regression models with party-by-wave interactions, controlling for age, race/ethnicity, Hispanic origin, gender, education, household size, congressional district partisan composition, and local mortality rates. We then extract estimated marginal means (emmeans) for each party at each wave, averaging over covariates. This approach isolates partisan differences while holding constant the demographic and geographic factors that might otherwise confound comparisons.

@fig-adjusted-behaviors displays the adjusted behavior levels by party across survey waves, and @fig-adjusted-gaps shows the resulting partisan gaps.

```{r}
#| label: fig-adjusted-behaviors
#| fig-cap: "Adjusted Health Behaviors and Perceptions for Republicans and Democrats Across Survey Waves. Points show estimated marginal means from models controlling for age, race/ethnicity, gender, education, household size, congressional district partisanship, and local mortality rates. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 14

library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Republican" = "#c50000", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Democrat" = "#326ba4")

# Contacts plot
p_contacts_adj <- ggplot(contacts_adjusted, aes(x = wave_label, y = estimate,
                                                 color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL), width = 0.15, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Contacts", title = "A. Adjusted Daily Contacts", color = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Masks plot
p_masks_adj <- ggplot(masks_adjusted, aes(x = wave_label, y = estimate,
                                           color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL), width = 0.15, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)", title = "B. Adjusted Mask Usage", color = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Concern plot
p_concern_adj <- ggplot(concern_adjusted, aes(x = wave_label, y = estimate,
                                               color = political_party, group = political_party)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL), width = 0.15, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Very Concerned (%)", title = "C. Adjusted Concern", color = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

p_contacts_adj + p_masks_adj + p_concern_adj + plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-adjusted-gaps
#| fig-cap: "Adjusted Partisan Gaps in Health Behaviors and Perceptions Over Time. Bars show the Republican-Democrat difference in estimated marginal means for daily contacts (Panel A), mask usage (Panel B), and stated concern (Panel C), after controlling for covariates. Negative values indicate Democrats are higher. Purple bars indicate statistically significant gaps (p < 0.05); gray bars indicate non-significant gaps."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 14

# Calculate standard errors for gaps and determine significance
# SE_gap = sqrt(SE_R^2 + SE_D^2)
calc_gap_significance <- function(behaviors_df, outcome_name) {
  behaviors_df %>%
    filter(outcome == outcome_name) %>%
    select(wave, political_party, estimate, std_error) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = political_party, values_from = c(estimate, std_error)) %>%
    mutate(
      gap_rep_dem = estimate_Republican - estimate_Democrat,
      se_gap = sqrt(std_error_Republican^2 + std_error_Democrat^2),
      significant = abs(gap_rep_dem) > 1.96 * se_gap
    ) %>%
    select(wave, gap_rep_dem, se_gap, significant)
}

contacts_sig <- calc_gap_significance(adjusted_behaviors, "contacts") %>%
  mutate(outcome_label = "A. Contact Gap (R - D)")
masks_sig <- calc_gap_significance(adjusted_behaviors, "masks") %>%
  mutate(outcome_label = "B. Mask Gap (R - D)")
concern_sig <- calc_gap_significance(adjusted_behaviors, "concern") %>%
  mutate(outcome_label = "C. Concern Gap (R - D)")

# Combine with wave labels
gaps_long <- bind_rows(contacts_sig, masks_sig, concern_sig) %>%
  mutate(
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

ggplot(gaps_long, aes(x = wave_label, y = gap_rep_dem, fill = significant)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", gap_rep_dem),
                y = gap_rep_dem / 2),
            color = "white", size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#7570b3", "FALSE" = "gray60"),
                    labels = c("TRUE" = "p < 0.05", "FALSE" = "n.s."),
                    name = NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~ outcome_label, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Adjusted R-D Difference") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.background = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

### Interpretation

After adjusting for covariates, the patterns of partisan differences reveal important nuances. For **contact behavior**, the descriptive data show that partisan gaps were smallest early in the pandemic, with Republicans reporting only 0.4 more contacts than Democrats in April 2020 compared to gaps of 1-3 contacts in later waves. The adjusted analysis goes further: after controlling for demographics, the April 2020 gap is not only small but *statistically indistinguishable from zero* (see @fig-adjusted-gaps, gray bars indicate non-significant differences). In fact, the point estimate is slightly negative, suggesting that if anything, Democrats had marginally more contacts than Republicans when mortality was at its peak. This stands in stark contrast to the large, statistically significant gaps observed in September 2020 and May 2021, periods of relatively lower pandemic intensity. The pattern supports the interpretation that concrete, visible threat can eliminate---not merely reduce---partisan divergence in behavior.

For **mask-wearing**, the adjusted analysis reveals that partisan differences fluctuated rather than monotonically increasing. The Democrat-Republican mask gap (with Democrats wearing masks more) ranged from `r sprintf("%.1f", abs(min_mask_gap_adj$gap_rep_dem))` percentage points in `r min_mask_gap_adj$wave_label` to `r sprintf("%.1f", abs(max_mask_gap_adj$gap_rep_dem))` percentage points in `r max_mask_gap_adj$wave_label`. Notably, the gap was relatively narrow during fall 2020 when mask usage was high across all groups, but widened again in early 2021 as vaccines became available and Republicans began to decrease mask usage more rapidly.

For **stated concern**, the adjusted analysis confirms that the perception gap was smallest at the beginning of the pandemic (when mortality was highest) and widened substantially thereafter. The adjusted Democrat-Republican concern gap ranged from `r sprintf("%.1f", abs(min_concern_gap_adj$gap_rep_dem))` percentage points in `r min_concern_gap_adj$wave_label` to `r sprintf("%.1f", abs(max_concern_gap_adj$gap_rep_dem))` percentage points in `r max_concern_gap_adj$wave_label`. This pattern parallels the contact findings: when objective threat was most severe, partisan gaps in both behavior and perception were smallest. The subsequent widening of the concern gap, even as mortality rates declined, supports the interpretation that politicization drove perceptual divergence.

These adjusted estimates confirm that partisan differences in health behaviors and perceptions were not simply artifacts of demographic composition or geographic sorting. Even after controlling for a comprehensive set of covariates, substantial partisan gaps persisted throughout the pandemic, though their magnitude varied with pandemic conditions---narrowing when mortality was highest and widening as the pandemic became increasingly politicized.

## Partisan Differences Within Policy Environments {#sec-policy-environment}

```{r}
#| label: load-policy-data
#| include: false

# Load behaviors by policy environment
behaviors_by_policy <- read_csv("../out/tables/behaviors_by_policy.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    policy = factor(policy, levels = c("No Mandate", "Less Strict", "Strict"))
  )
```

A potential alternative explanation for partisan differences in health behaviors is that Democrats disproportionately reside in jurisdictions with stricter COVID-19 policies, and their behavior simply reflects compliance with local regulations rather than partisan preferences. To address this concern, we examine whether partisan differences persist within the same policy environment.

@fig-contacts-by-policy and @fig-masks-by-policy display mean contact rates and mask usage by party, stratified by county-level mask mandate policy (No Mandate, Less Strict, or Strict). If policy environment fully explained partisan differences, we would expect the gaps between parties to disappear within each policy category.

```{r}
#| label: fig-contacts-by-policy
#| fig-cap: "Daily Contacts by Party Within Policy Environments. Bars show weighted mean daily contacts for each party, faceted by county mask mandate policy. Error bars indicate standard errors."
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 6

library(cowplot)

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Republican" = "#c50000", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Democrat" = "#326ba4")

# Contacts by policy - faceted vertically
ggplot(behaviors_by_policy, aes(x = political_party, y = contacts_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = contacts_mean - contacts_se, ymax = contacts_mean + contacts_se),
                width = 0.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", contacts_mean)), vjust = -1.2, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ policy, ncol = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Contacts") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 11),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    strip.background = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-masks-by-policy
#| fig-cap: "Mask Usage by Party Within Policy Environments. Bars show weighted mean mask usage (percentage of contacts with masks) for each party, faceted by county mask mandate policy. Error bars indicate standard errors."
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 6

# Masks by policy - faceted vertically
ggplot(behaviors_by_policy, aes(x = political_party, y = mask_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mask_mean - mask_se, ymax = mask_mean + mask_se),
                width = 0.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.0f%%", mask_mean)), vjust = -1.2, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ policy, ncol = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2)), limits = c(0, 85)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 11),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    strip.background = element_blank()
  )
```

The results clearly demonstrate that partisan differences are not simply artifacts of differential policy exposure. Within each policy environment, Republicans reported more daily contacts than Democrats: the gap was 1.4 contacts in No Mandate areas, 1.0 contacts in Less Strict areas, and 1.3 contacts in Strict mandate areas. Similarly, Democrats reported higher mask usage than Republicans across all policy contexts: the gap was 9 percentage points in No Mandate areas, 11 percentage points in Less Strict areas, and 10 percentage points in Strict mandate areas.

These findings suggest that partisan identity shapes health behaviors above and beyond the effects of local policy. While stricter policies are associated with higher mask usage overall, the Republican-Democrat gap in mask-wearing remains remarkably stable across policy environments. This pattern is consistent with partisanship operating through mechanisms other than policy compliance---such as differential risk perception, social norms within partisan networks, or ideological commitments---that persist regardless of the local regulatory context.

## Robustness to Context and Demographic Controls {#sec-context-demographics-robustness}

This section presents a formal test of whether partisan gaps in health behaviors can be explained by local context (metropolitan status, county mask mandates) or demographic composition (sex, race, age, education). We estimate partisan gaps by census region using three nested models and examine whether gaps shrink toward zero as controls are added.

```{r}
#| label: fig-region-gap-context-adjustment
#| fig-cap: "Effect of Controlling for Local Context and Demographics on Regional Partisan Gaps. Top row shows the absolute partisan gap in daily contacts (N = 12,753); bottom row shows the absolute partisan gap in mask usage (N = 9,087). Sample restricted to Democrats and Republicans; mask usage sample is smaller because it is conditional on reporting at least one contact with mask information. Each panel represents a census region. Points show gaps from three nested models: Baseline (wave + mortality), + Context (adds metro status and county mask mandates), and + Demographics (adds sex, race, age, and education). If context or demographics explain partisan gaps, the gaps should shrink toward zero as controls are added."
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 12

library(emmeans)
library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)

# Load data
bics <- read_csv("../data/BICS_ZIP_Features.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Define census regions from STATE
northeast <- c("Connecticut", "Maine", "Massachusetts", "New Hampshire", "Rhode Island",
               "Vermont", "New Jersey", "New York", "Pennsylvania")
midwest <- c("Illinois", "Indiana", "Michigan", "Ohio", "Wisconsin",
             "Iowa", "Kansas", "Minnesota", "Missouri", "Nebraska", "North Dakota", "South Dakota")
south <- c("Delaware", "Florida", "Georgia", "Maryland", "North Carolina", "South Carolina",
           "Virginia", "District of Columbia", "West Virginia", "Alabama", "Kentucky",
           "Mississippi", "Tennessee", "Arkansas", "Louisiana", "Oklahoma", "Texas")
west <- c("Arizona", "Colorado", "Idaho", "Montana", "Nevada", "New Mexico", "Utah",
          "Wyoming", "Alaska", "California", "Hawaii", "Oregon", "Washington")

# Add census region to full dataset
bics <- bics %>%
  mutate(
    census_region = case_when(
      STATE %in% northeast ~ "Northeast",
      STATE %in% midwest ~ "Midwest",
      STATE %in% south ~ "South",
      STATE %in% west ~ "West",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )

# Prepare CONTACTS data (larger sample - doesn't require mask data)
bics_contacts <- bics %>%
  filter(!is.na(num_cc), !is.na(census_region), !is.na(political_party),
         !is.na(log_prev_week_mort_rate), !is.na(Metro), !is.na(wave),
         !is.na(county_mask_mandate),
         !is.na(resp_sex), !is.na(r_race), !is.na(age_group), !is.na(resp_educ)) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    wave = factor(wave),
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    census_region = factor(census_region, levels = c("South", "Northeast", "Midwest", "West")),
    county_mask_mandate = factor(county_mask_mandate)
  )

# Prepare MASKS data (smaller sample - requires reported contacts with mask info)
bics_masks <- bics %>%
  filter(!is.na(Norm_Masks_Used), !is.na(census_region), !is.na(political_party),
         !is.na(log_prev_week_mort_rate), !is.na(Metro), !is.na(wave),
         !is.na(county_mask_mandate),
         !is.na(resp_sex), !is.na(r_race), !is.na(age_group), !is.na(resp_educ)) %>%
  filter(political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican")) %>%
  mutate(
    wave = factor(wave),
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    census_region = factor(census_region, levels = c("South", "Northeast", "Midwest", "West")),
    county_mask_mandate = factor(county_mask_mandate)
  )

# Fit three nested models for CONTACTS
contact_m1 <- lm(num_cc ~ political_party * census_region + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate,
                 data = bics_contacts, weights = weight_pooled)
contact_m2 <- lm(num_cc ~ political_party * census_region + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                   Metro + county_mask_mandate,
                 data = bics_contacts, weights = weight_pooled)
contact_m3 <- lm(num_cc ~ political_party * census_region + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                   Metro + county_mask_mandate +
                   resp_sex + r_race + age_group + resp_educ,
                 data = bics_contacts, weights = weight_pooled)

# Fit three nested models for MASKS
mask_m1 <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ political_party * census_region + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate,
              data = bics_masks, weights = weight_pooled)
mask_m2 <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ political_party * census_region + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                Metro + county_mask_mandate,
              data = bics_masks, weights = weight_pooled)
mask_m3 <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ political_party * census_region + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                Metro + county_mask_mandate +
                resp_sex + r_race + age_group + resp_educ,
              data = bics_masks, weights = weight_pooled)

# Function to extract partisan gaps with nuisance factors
get_gaps <- function(model, model_name, nuisance_vars = NULL) {
  if (is.null(nuisance_vars)) {
    emm <- emmeans(model, ~ political_party | census_region)
  } else {
    emm <- emmeans(model, ~ political_party | census_region, nuisance = nuisance_vars)
  }
  contrast(emm, method = "revpairwise") %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    mutate(model = model_name)
}

# Extract gaps for all models
contact_gaps <- bind_rows(
  get_gaps(contact_m1, "Baseline"),
  get_gaps(contact_m2, "+ Context", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate")),
  get_gaps(contact_m3, "+ Demographics", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate",
                                            "resp_sex", "r_race", "age_group", "resp_educ"))
) %>%
  mutate(
    model = factor(model, levels = c("Baseline", "+ Context", "+ Demographics")),
    census_region = factor(census_region, levels = c("South", "Northeast", "Midwest", "West"))
  )

mask_gaps <- bind_rows(
  get_gaps(mask_m1, "Baseline"),
  get_gaps(mask_m2, "+ Context", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate")),
  get_gaps(mask_m3, "+ Demographics", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate",
                                         "resp_sex", "r_race", "age_group", "resp_educ"))
) %>%
  mutate(
    model = factor(model, levels = c("Baseline", "+ Context", "+ Demographics")),
    census_region = factor(census_region, levels = c("South", "Northeast", "Midwest", "West")),
    # Flip sign so positive = Democrats mask more
    estimate_dr = -estimate,
    lower = -estimate - SE,
    upper = -estimate + SE
  )

# Model colors
model_colors <- c("Baseline" = "#2171b5", "+ Context" = "#d95f02", "+ Demographics" = "#1b9e77")

# Create contact gap plots (one per region) - no x-axis ticks (traced from bottom row)
contact_plots <- map(levels(contact_gaps$census_region), function(region) {
  df <- contact_gaps %>% filter(census_region == region)
  ggplot(df, aes(x = model, y = abs(estimate), color = model)) +
    geom_point(size = 3.5) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = abs(estimate) - SE, ymax = abs(estimate) + SE), width = 0.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    scale_color_manual(values = model_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 2.5)) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = if(region == "South") "Partisan Gap\n(contacts)" else NULL,
         title = region) +
    theme_cowplot() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, hjust = 0.5),
      axis.text.x = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
    )
})

# Create mask gap plots (one per region) - with x-axis ticks
mask_plots <- map(levels(mask_gaps$census_region), function(region) {
  df <- mask_gaps %>% filter(census_region == region)
  ggplot(df, aes(x = model, y = abs(estimate_dr), color = model)) +
    geom_point(size = 3.5) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = abs(estimate_dr) - SE, ymax = abs(estimate_dr) + SE), width = 0.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    scale_color_manual(values = model_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 18)) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = if(region == "South") "Partisan Gap\n(mask usage)" else NULL,
         title = NULL) +
    theme_cowplot() +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 11)
    )
})

# Combine into 2 rows × 4 columns
# Add row labels
contact_row <- wrap_plots(contact_plots, nrow = 1) +
  plot_annotation(subtitle = "A. Daily Contacts")
mask_row <- wrap_plots(mask_plots, nrow = 1) +
  plot_annotation(subtitle = "B. Mask Usage")

contact_row / mask_row
```

The results show that partisan gaps in both contacts and mask usage remain substantial even after controlling for local context and demographics. For contacts, the gaps are remarkably stable across specifications, suggesting that neither urban-rural differences, policy environments, nor demographic composition can explain partisan behavioral differences. For masks, gaps shrink modestly---particularly in the South---but persist in all regions even in the fully adjusted model, with gaps ranging from approximately 4 percentage points (South) to 12 percentage points (West).

### Partisan Gaps by Congressional District Context {#sec-cd-context-gaps}

@fig-cd-context-gaps presents a complementary view of the main text analysis (@fig-cd-context-emmeans), showing the absolute Republican-Democrat gaps in contacts and mask usage by congressional district partisan context across the three model specifications.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cd-context-gaps
#| fig-cap: "Partisan Gaps by Local Partisan Context Across Model Specifications. Left column shows gaps in Democratic-led congressional districts; right column shows gaps in Republican-led districts. Top row shows the absolute partisan gap in daily contacts; bottom row shows the absolute partisan gap in mask usage. Points show gaps from three nested models: Baseline (wave + mortality), + Context (adds metro status and county mask mandates), and + Demographics (adds sex, race, age, and education). Sample restricted to Democrats and Republicans."
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

library(emmeans)
library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)

# Load data
bics <- read_csv("../data/BICS_ZIP_Features.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Create party and district variables
bics_cd <- bics %>%
  filter(!is.na(political_party_to_CD)) %>%
  mutate(
    party = case_when(
      grepl("^Democrat", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Democrat",
      grepl("^Republican", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Republican",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    district = case_when(
      grepl("Democratic led", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Democratic District",
      grepl("Republican led", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Republican District",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(party), !is.na(district))

# Prepare CONTACTS data
bics_cd_contacts <- bics_cd %>%
  filter(!is.na(num_cc), !is.na(log_prev_week_mort_rate), !is.na(Metro), !is.na(wave),
         !is.na(county_mask_mandate),
         !is.na(resp_sex), !is.na(r_race), !is.na(age_group), !is.na(resp_educ)) %>%
  mutate(
    wave = factor(wave),
    party = factor(party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    district = factor(district, levels = c("Democratic District", "Republican District")),
    county_mask_mandate = factor(county_mask_mandate)
  )

# Prepare MASKS data
bics_cd_masks <- bics_cd %>%
  filter(!is.na(Norm_Masks_Used), !is.na(log_prev_week_mort_rate), !is.na(Metro), !is.na(wave),
         !is.na(county_mask_mandate),
         !is.na(resp_sex), !is.na(r_race), !is.na(age_group), !is.na(resp_educ)) %>%
  mutate(
    wave = factor(wave),
    party = factor(party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    district = factor(district, levels = c("Democratic District", "Republican District")),
    county_mask_mandate = factor(county_mask_mandate)
  )

# Fit three nested models for CONTACTS
contact_m1 <- lm(num_cc ~ party * district + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate,
                 data = bics_cd_contacts, weights = weight_pooled)
contact_m2 <- lm(num_cc ~ party * district + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                   Metro + county_mask_mandate,
                 data = bics_cd_contacts, weights = weight_pooled)
contact_m3 <- lm(num_cc ~ party * district + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                   Metro + county_mask_mandate +
                   resp_sex + r_race + age_group + resp_educ,
                 data = bics_cd_contacts, weights = weight_pooled)

# Fit three nested models for MASKS
mask_m1 <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ party * district + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate,
              data = bics_cd_masks, weights = weight_pooled)
mask_m2 <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ party * district + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                Metro + county_mask_mandate,
              data = bics_cd_masks, weights = weight_pooled)
mask_m3 <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ party * district + wave + log_prev_week_mort_rate +
                Metro + county_mask_mandate +
                resp_sex + r_race + age_group + resp_educ,
              data = bics_cd_masks, weights = weight_pooled)

# Function to extract partisan gaps by district
get_gaps_by_district <- function(model, model_name, nuisance_vars = NULL) {
  if (is.null(nuisance_vars)) {
    emm <- emmeans(model, ~ party | district)
  } else {
    emm <- emmeans(model, ~ party | district, nuisance = nuisance_vars)
  }
  contrast(emm, method = "revpairwise") %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    mutate(model = model_name)
}

# Extract gaps for all models
contact_gaps <- bind_rows(
  get_gaps_by_district(contact_m1, "Baseline"),
  get_gaps_by_district(contact_m2, "+ Context", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate")),
  get_gaps_by_district(contact_m3, "+ Demographics", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate",
                                                        "resp_sex", "r_race", "age_group", "resp_educ"))
) %>%
  mutate(
    model = factor(model, levels = c("Baseline", "+ Context", "+ Demographics")),
    district = factor(district, levels = c("Democratic District", "Republican District"))
  )

mask_gaps <- bind_rows(
  get_gaps_by_district(mask_m1, "Baseline"),
  get_gaps_by_district(mask_m2, "+ Context", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate")),
  get_gaps_by_district(mask_m3, "+ Demographics", c("wave", "Metro", "county_mask_mandate",
                                                     "resp_sex", "r_race", "age_group", "resp_educ"))
) %>%
  mutate(
    model = factor(model, levels = c("Baseline", "+ Context", "+ Demographics")),
    district = factor(district, levels = c("Democratic District", "Republican District")),
    estimate_dr = -estimate,
    lower = -estimate - SE,
    upper = -estimate + SE
  )

# Model colors
model_colors <- c("Baseline" = "#2171b5", "+ Context" = "#d95f02", "+ Demographics" = "#1b9e77")

# Create contact gap plots by district - no x-axis ticks
contact_plots <- map(levels(contact_gaps$district), function(dist) {
  df <- contact_gaps %>% filter(district == dist)
  ggplot(df, aes(x = model, y = abs(estimate), color = model)) +
    geom_point(size = 3.5) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = abs(estimate) - SE, ymax = abs(estimate) + SE), width = 0.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    scale_color_manual(values = model_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 3)) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = if(dist == "Democratic District") "Partisan Gap\n(contacts)" else NULL,
         title = dist) +
    theme_cowplot() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
      axis.text.x = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
    )
})

# Create mask gap plots by district - with x-axis ticks
mask_plots <- map(levels(mask_gaps$district), function(dist) {
  df <- mask_gaps %>% filter(district == dist)
  ggplot(df, aes(x = model, y = abs(estimate_dr), color = model)) +
    geom_point(size = 3.5) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = abs(estimate_dr) - SE, ymax = abs(estimate_dr) + SE), width = 0.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    scale_color_manual(values = model_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 18)) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = if(dist == "Democratic District") "Partisan Gap\n(mask usage)" else NULL,
         title = NULL) +
    theme_cowplot() +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 11)
    )
})

# Combine: rows = behavior, columns = district context
contact_row <- wrap_plots(contact_plots, nrow = 1)
mask_row <- wrap_plots(mask_plots, nrow = 1)

contact_row / mask_row
```

The gaps remain stable across model specifications in both district contexts. In Democratic-led districts, the contact gap ranges from 1.4-1.6 contacts across specifications; in Republican-led districts, it ranges from 0.5-0.7 contacts. For masks, gaps in Democratic districts range from 7-8 percentage points, while gaps in Republican districts range from 6-8 percentage points. These results complement the main text finding that partisan gaps persist regardless of local partisan context.

### Robustness to Model Specification {#sec-cd-context-robustness}

@fig-cd-context-emmeans displays predicted health behaviors by party and congressional district context, showing that partisan gaps persist in both Democratic-led and Republican-led districts.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cd-context-emmeans
#| fig-cap: "Predicted Health Behaviors by Party and Congressional District Context. Panels A-B show predicted daily contacts; Panels C-D show predicted mask usage. Left column shows Democratic-led districts; right column shows Republican-led districts. Estimates are adjusted for local incidence, mask mandates, metro status, and demographics. Partisan gaps persist in both district contexts."
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 9

(header_dem | header_rep) / (p_a | p_b) / (p_c | p_d) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 1, 1))
```

@fig-cd-context-robustness demonstrates that partisan gaps in health behaviors persist regardless of whether we control for local context and demographic characteristics. The figure compares unadjusted estimates (wave only) with adjusted estimates (controlling for incidence, mask mandates, metro status, and demographics).

```{r}
#| label: fig-cd-context-robustness
#| fig-cap: "Robustness of Partisan Gaps to Model Specification. Panels A-B show predicted daily contacts; Panels C-D show predicted mask usage. Left column shows Democratic-led districts; right column shows Republican-led districts. Open circles show unadjusted estimates (wave only); filled circles show adjusted estimates (controlling for incidence, mask mandates, metro status, demographics). Partisan gaps persist regardless of model specification."
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 9

library(emmeans)
library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)

# Load data
bics <- read_csv("../data/BICS_ZIP_Features.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Filter to valid CD data (Democrats and Republicans only for cleaner comparison)
filtered_df <- bics %>%
  filter(!is.na(political_party) & !is.na(CONGRESSPERSON_PARTY) &
         political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Republican"))

# Define factor levels for CD context
cd_levels <- c("Democrat in Democratic led CD",
               "Democrat in Republican led CD",
               "Republican in Democratic led CD",
               "Republican in Republican led CD")

# =============================================================================
# CONTACTS
# =============================================================================

# Adjusted model
cd_contact_model <- lm(num_cc ~ log_prev_week_inc_rate + county_mask_mandate + Metro +
                         male + resp_educ + resp_yob + resp_hhsize + r_race + r_working +
                         wave + political_party_to_CD,
                       data = filtered_df,
                       weights = weight_pooled)

emm_contact_adj <- emmeans(cd_contact_model, specs = "political_party_to_CD",
                           nuisance = c("log_prev_week_inc_rate", "county_mask_mandate", "Metro",
                                        "male", "resp_educ", "resp_yob", "resp_hhsize", "r_race",
                                        "r_working", "wave"))
emm_contact_adj <- as.data.frame(summary(emm_contact_adj)) %>%
  filter(political_party_to_CD %in% cd_levels) %>%
  mutate(controls = "Adjusted")

# Unadjusted model (wave only)
cd_contact_model_raw <- lm(num_cc ~ wave + political_party_to_CD,
                           data = filtered_df,
                           weights = weight_pooled)
emm_contact_raw <- emmeans(cd_contact_model_raw, specs = "political_party_to_CD",
                           nuisance = "wave")
emm_contact_raw <- as.data.frame(summary(emm_contact_raw)) %>%
  filter(political_party_to_CD %in% cd_levels) %>%
  mutate(controls = "Unadjusted")

all_contact_emms <- rbind(emm_contact_adj, emm_contact_raw)

# =============================================================================
# MASKS
# =============================================================================

# Adjusted model
cd_mask_model <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ log_prev_week_inc_rate + county_mask_mandate + Metro +
                      male + resp_educ + resp_yob + resp_hhsize + r_race + r_working +
                      wave + political_party_to_CD,
                    data = filtered_df,
                    weights = weight_pooled)

emm_mask_adj <- emmeans(cd_mask_model, specs = "political_party_to_CD",
                        nuisance = c("log_prev_week_inc_rate", "county_mask_mandate", "Metro",
```
