```{r}
                                     "male", "resp_educ", "resp_yob", "resp_hhsize", "r_race",
                                     "r_working", "wave"))
emm_mask_adj <- as.data.frame(summary(emm_mask_adj)) %>%
  filter(political_party_to_CD %in% cd_levels) %>%
  mutate(controls = "Adjusted")

# Unadjusted model (wave only)
cd_mask_model_raw <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ wave + political_party_to_CD,
                        data = filtered_df,
                        weights = weight_pooled)
emm_mask_raw <- emmeans(cd_mask_model_raw, specs = "political_party_to_CD",
                        nuisance = "wave")
emm_mask_raw <- as.data.frame(summary(emm_mask_raw)) %>%
  filter(political_party_to_CD %in% cd_levels) %>%
  mutate(controls = "Unadjusted")

all_mask_emms <- rbind(emm_mask_adj, emm_mask_raw)

# =============================================================================
# PLOTS - 2x2 layout with individual subplot labels
# =============================================================================

# Parse party and district context
parse_cd_data <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(
      party = case_when(
        grepl("^Democrat", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Democrat",
        grepl("^Republican", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Republican"
      ),
      district_context = case_when(
        grepl("Democratic led CD", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Democratic District",
        grepl("Republican led CD", political_party_to_CD) ~ "Republican District"
      ),
      party = factor(party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
      district_context = factor(district_context, levels = c("Democratic District", "Republican District"))
    )
}

all_contact_emms <- parse_cd_data(all_contact_emms)
all_mask_emms <- parse_cd_data(all_mask_emms)

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Democrat" = "#2166AC", "Republican" = "#B2182B")

# Fixed y-axis limits for comparability (same as main figure)
contacts_ylim <- c(4, 7.5)
masks_ylim <- c(40, 80)

# Common theme with subtle horizontal gridlines
theme_grid <- theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "gray85", linewidth = 0.3)
  )

# A: Contacts in Democratic District
p_a <- ggplot(all_contact_emms %>% filter(district_context == "Democratic District"),
              aes(x = party, y = emmean, color = party,
                  group = interaction(party, controls))) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = controls), size = 4, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = emmean - SE, ymax = emmean + SE),
                width = 0.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Unadjusted" = 1, "Adjusted" = 16)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = contacts_ylim) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Predicted Daily Contacts", title = "A. Daily Contacts") +
  theme_grid +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# B: Contacts in Republican District
p_b <- ggplot(all_contact_emms %>% filter(district_context == "Republican District"),
              aes(x = party, y = emmean, color = party,
                  group = interaction(party, controls))) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = controls), size = 4, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = emmean - SE, ymax = emmean + SE),
                width = 0.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Unadjusted" = 1, "Adjusted" = 16)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = contacts_ylim) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "B. Daily Contacts") +
  theme_grid +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# C: Masks in Democratic District
p_c <- ggplot(all_mask_emms %>% filter(district_context == "Democratic District"),
              aes(x = party, y = emmean, color = party,
                  group = interaction(party, controls))) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = controls), size = 4, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = emmean - SE, ymax = emmean + SE),
                width = 0.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Unadjusted" = 1, "Adjusted" = 16)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = masks_ylim) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Predicted Mask Usage (%)", title = "C. Mask Usage") +
  theme_grid +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# D: Masks in Republican District
p_d <- ggplot(all_mask_emms %>% filter(district_context == "Republican District"),
              aes(x = party, y = emmean, color = party,
                  group = interaction(party, controls))) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = controls), size = 4, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = emmean - SE, ymax = emmean + SE),
                width = 0.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Unadjusted" = 1, "Adjusted" = 16)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = masks_ylim) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "D. Mask Usage") +
  theme_grid +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Create column header labels
header_dem <- wrap_elements(grid::textGrob("Democratic District",
                                            gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 14, fontface = "bold")))
header_rep <- wrap_elements(grid::textGrob("Republican District",
                                            gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 14, fontface = "bold")))

# Combine: header row + contacts row + masks row
(header_dem | header_rep) / (p_a | p_b) / (p_c | p_d) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 1, 1))
```

The adjustment for context and demographics has minimal impact on partisan gaps. For contacts, the Republican-Democrat gap shifts only slightly when adding controls: in Democratic districts, the gap remains approximately 1.3-1.5 contacts whether unadjusted or adjusted; in Republican districts, the gap remains approximately 0.5-0.8 contacts. For masks, the Democratic advantage over Republicans remains stable at approximately 7-10 percentage points across both district contexts and model specifications. This stability indicates that partisan differences in health behaviors are not explained by compositional differences or local contextual factors.

## Weight Sensitivity Analysis {#sec-weight-sensitivity}

```{r}
#| label: load-sensitivity-data
#| include: false

# Load sensitivity analysis results
# Use relative paths from paper directory
weight_summary <- read_csv("../out/tables/weight_sensitivity_summary.csv",
                           show_col_types = FALSE)
regression_sensitivity <- read_csv("../out/tables/regression_weight_sensitivity.csv",
                                    show_col_types = FALSE)

# Extract key values for inline reporting
contact_gap_unweighted <- weight_summary %>%
  filter(Statistic == "Contact Gap (R-D)") %>%
  pull(Unweighted)
contact_gap_weighted <- weight_summary %>%
  filter(Statistic == "Contact Gap (R-D)") %>%
  pull(Original)
contact_gap_pct_change <- abs((contact_gap_weighted - contact_gap_unweighted) / contact_gap_unweighted) * 100

vax_gap_unweighted <- weight_summary %>%
  filter(Statistic == "Vaccination Gap (R-D)") %>%
  pull(Unweighted)
vax_gap_weighted <- weight_summary %>%
  filter(Statistic == "Vaccination Gap (R-D)") %>%
  pull(Original)
vax_gap_pct_change <- abs((vax_gap_weighted - vax_gap_unweighted) / vax_gap_unweighted) * 100

mask_gap_unweighted <- regression_sensitivity %>%
  filter(outcome == "Mask Usage", comparison == "Republican - Democrat", weight_type == "Unweighted") %>%
  pull(estimate)
mask_gap_weighted <- regression_sensitivity %>%
  filter(outcome == "Mask Usage", comparison == "Republican - Democrat", weight_type == "Original") %>%
  pull(estimate)
```

To assess the robustness of our findings to different weighting approaches, we compare results across three scenarios: (1) unweighted estimates, (2) original demographic weights that adjust for age, gender, race/ethnicity, and education, and (3) raked weights that additionally adjust for party identification to match national targets from Gallup polling (30% Democrat, 29% Republican, 39% Independent; @gallup_party_2021). The raking procedure adjusts for potential correlations between party identification and demographic characteristics in our sample.

A key finding from this analysis is that raked weights and original demographic weights produce identical within-party estimates for all outcomes. This equivalence occurs because the raking procedure applies a constant multiplier to all observations within each party group. When calculating within-party means or party-specific regression coefficients, this constant multiplier cancels out. Raking only matters for population-level estimates that pool across partisan groups, where it adjusts the relative contribution of each party to the overall estimate.

We observe small differences between unweighted and weighted estimates. The Republican-Democrat contact gap is `r sprintf("%.2f", contact_gap_unweighted)` contacts per day using unweighted data compared to `r sprintf("%.2f", contact_gap_weighted)` contacts per day with demographic weights---a `r sprintf("%.0f", contact_gap_pct_change)`% reduction. The vaccine acceptance gap is `r sprintf("%.1f", vax_gap_unweighted)` percentage points (unweighted) versus `r sprintf("%.1f", vax_gap_weighted)` percentage points (weighted)---a `r sprintf("%.0f", vax_gap_pct_change)`% reduction in the absolute gap. Mask usage differences remain stable across all weighting scenarios, with the Republican-Democrat gap at approximately `r sprintf("%.1f", mask_gap_unweighted)` percentage points regardless of weights used.

Importantly, all partisan differences remain highly significant (p < 0.001) across all three weighting scenarios. These results demonstrate that our findings are robust to the choice of weighting specification and that the substantive conclusions about partisan gaps in health behaviors do not depend on how we adjust for sample composition.

### Summary Table

```{r}
#| label: tbl-weight-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: "Partisan Differences in Health Behaviors Across Weighting Scenarios"

# Create summary table for key partisan gaps
sensitivity_table <- regression_sensitivity %>%
  filter(comparison %in% c("Republican - Democrat", "Independent - Democrat")) %>%
  select(weight_type, comparison, estimate, std.error, sig, outcome) %>%
  mutate(
    weight_type = factor(weight_type, levels = c("Unweighted", "Original", "Raked")),
    comparison = factor(comparison, levels = c("Republican - Democrat", "Independent - Democrat")),
    outcome = factor(outcome, levels = c("Contacts", "Mask Usage", "Vaccine Acceptance")),
    estimate_se = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f)%s", estimate, std.error, sig)
  ) %>%
  select(outcome, comparison, weight_type, estimate_se) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = weight_type,
    values_from = estimate_se
  ) %>%
  arrange(outcome, comparison)

# Format table
kable(sensitivity_table,
      col.names = c("Outcome", "Comparison", "Unweighted", "Original Weights", "Raked Weights"),
      align = c("l", "l", "c", "c", "c"),
      booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position")) %>%
  footnote(general = "Estimates shown with standard errors in parentheses. Significance: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001. Contacts measured as daily contacts; mask usage and vaccine acceptance measured in percentage points.",
           general_title = "Note: ",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
```

### Comparison of Partisan Gaps

```{r}
#| label: fig-weight-sensitivity
#| fig-cap: "Republican-Democrat Differences in Health Behaviors Across Weighting Scenarios"
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 8

# Prepare data for plotting
plot_data <- regression_sensitivity %>%
  filter(comparison == "Republican - Democrat") %>%
  mutate(
    weight_type = factor(weight_type, levels = c("Unweighted", "Original", "Raked")),
    outcome = factor(outcome, levels = c("Contacts", "Mask Usage", "Vaccine Acceptance"),
                     labels = c("Daily Contacts\n(count)",
                               "Mask Usage\n(percentage points)",
                               "Vaccine Acceptance\n(percentage points)"))
  )

library(cowplot)

# Create grouped bar chart
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = outcome, y = estimate, fill = weight_type)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7,
           alpha = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
                    ymax = estimate + 1.96 * std.error),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
                width = 0.25) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Unweighted" = "#1b9e77",
                               "Original" = "#d95f02",
                               "Raked" = "#7570b3"),
                    name = "Weighting\nScenario") +
  labs(x = NULL,
       y = "Republican - Democrat Difference") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10)
  )
```

## Geographic Variation in Incidence Responsiveness {#sec-geographic-variation}

```{r}
#| label: load-wave-responsiveness
#| include: false

# Load wave-by-wave incidence responsiveness results
wave_resp <- read_csv("../out/tables/wave_incidence_responsiveness.csv",
                      show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

# Summary statistics
n_waves_significant <- sum(wave_resp$gap_change < 0, na.rm = TRUE)
avg_gap_change <- mean(wave_resp$gap_change, na.rm = TRUE)
max_rep_reduction <- wave_resp %>% slice_min(effect_rep, n = 1)
min_rep_reduction <- wave_resp %>% slice_max(effect_rep, n = 1)
```

The main text presents models that pool across survey waves, using temporal variation in pandemic conditions (alongside geographic variation) to identify behavioral responses. To isolate the role of geographic variation, we estimate separate models for each survey wave. Within a single wave, variation in county-level incidence rates is purely geographic, reflecting cross-sectional differences in local pandemic severity rather than temporal trends. This approach allows us to examine whether partisan differences in responsiveness to local conditions are consistent across different periods of the pandemic.

@tbl-wave-responsiveness presents the estimated change in daily contacts associated with a 10% increase in local (log) incidence rates, by party and wave. All models include state fixed effects to control for time-invariant state characteristics. @fig-wave-responsiveness visualizes these results, showing how the incidence-responsiveness of each partisan group varied over the study period.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-wave-responsiveness
#| tbl-cap: "Contact Response to Local Incidence by Wave and Party"

wave_resp %>%
  select(wave_label, n, mean_inc_rate, effect_dem, effect_ind, effect_rep, gap_change) %>%
  kable(
    col.names = c("Wave", "N", "Mean Inc. Rate", "Democrat", "Independent", "Republican", "R-D Gap Change"),
    digits = c(0, 0, 1, 3, 3, 3, 3),
    align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
    booktabs = TRUE
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "scale_down")) %>%
  footnote(
    general = "Effects represent the change in daily contacts associated with a 10% increase in local log incidence rate. Negative values indicate contact reduction. R-D Gap Change shows how the Republican-Democrat contact gap changes (negative = gap narrows). All models include state fixed effects, city indicators, and interview day controls.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-wave-responsiveness
#| fig-cap: "Contact Response to Local Incidence Across Survey Waves. Points show the estimated change in daily contacts associated with a 10% increase in local log incidence rates, by party and wave. Error bars indicate standard errors. Dashed line at zero represents no response."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 9

# Party colors (matching other BICS plots)
party_colors <- c("Democrat" = "#326ba4", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Republican" = "#c50000")

# Reshape for plotting
wave_plot_data <- wave_resp %>%
  select(wave_label, effect_dem, effect_ind, effect_rep, se_dem, se_ind, se_rep) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("effect_"),
    names_to = "party",
    names_prefix = "effect_",
    values_to = "effect"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    se = case_when(
      party == "dem" ~ wave_resp$se_dem[match(wave_label, wave_resp$wave_label)],
      party == "ind" ~ wave_resp$se_ind[match(wave_label, wave_resp$wave_label)],
      party == "rep" ~ wave_resp$se_rep[match(wave_label, wave_resp$wave_label)]
    ),
    party = case_when(
      party == "dem" ~ "Democrat",
      party == "ind" ~ "Independent",
      party == "rep" ~ "Republican"
    ),
    party = factor(party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat"))
  )

ggplot(wave_plot_data, aes(x = wave_label, y = effect, color = party, group = party)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = effect - se, ymax = effect + se), width = 0.15) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors) +
  labs(
    x = "Survey Wave",
    y = "Change in Daily Contacts\n(per 10% increase in log incidence)",
    color = "Party"
  ) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )
```

### Interpretation

The wave-by-wave analysis reveals several patterns. First, Republicans consistently show stronger contact reductions in response to local incidence than Democrats across most survey waves. In `r n_waves_significant` of 6 waves, the Republican-Democrat gap change is negative, indicating that higher local incidence narrows the partisan contact gap. Second, the magnitude of responsiveness varies over time, with the strongest Republican responses occurring during `r max_rep_reduction$wave_label` when mean incidence was `r sprintf("%.1f", max_rep_reduction$mean_inc_rate)` cases per 100,000.

These within-wave analyses provide complementary evidence to the pooled models in the main text. By isolating geographic variation in incidence, we demonstrate that partisan differences in responsiveness are not artifacts of differential exposure to temporal trends in the pandemic. Rather, when comparing individuals facing different local conditions at the same point in time, Republicans are more responsive to higher local incidence than Democrats---a pattern that helps explain the narrowing of partisan gaps during periods of elevated disease burden.

## Partisan Behavior Levels Over Time {#sec-behavior-levels}

```{r}
#| label: load-behavior-levels
#| include: false

# Load wave-level behavior data
behaviors_by_wave <- read_csv("../out/tables/behaviors_by_wave.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    wave_label = case_when(
      wave == 1 ~ "Apr '20",
      wave == 2 ~ "Jun '20",
      wave == 3 ~ "Sep '20",
      wave == 4 ~ "Dec '20",
      wave == 5 ~ "Feb '21",
      wave == 6 ~ "May '21"
    ),
    wave_label = factor(wave_label, levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
  )

# Calculate variability statistics
contact_variability <- behaviors_by_wave %>%
  group_by(political_party) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_contacts = mean(total_contacts),
    sd_contacts = sd(total_contacts),
    cv_contacts = sd(total_contacts) / mean(total_contacts) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

mask_variability <- behaviors_by_wave %>%
  group_by(political_party) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_mask = mean(mask_usage),
    sd_mask = sd(mask_usage),
    cv_mask = sd(mask_usage) / mean(mask_usage) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

rep_cv_contacts <- contact_variability %>% filter(political_party == "Republican") %>% pull(cv_contacts)
dem_cv_contacts <- contact_variability %>% filter(political_party == "Democrat") %>% pull(cv_contacts)
rep_cv_mask <- mask_variability %>% filter(political_party == "Republican") %>% pull(cv_mask)
dem_cv_mask <- mask_variability %>% filter(political_party == "Democrat") %>% pull(cv_mask)
```

The main text documents how partisan gaps in health behaviors varied over the course of the pandemic. To understand what drives these fluctuations, @fig-behavior-levels displays the average behavior levels for each party across survey waves. This decomposition reveals an important asymmetry: **Democrats maintained relatively stable behavior throughout the pandemic, while Republicans showed substantially greater variability**.

```{r}
#| label: fig-behavior-levels
#| fig-cap: "Health Behavior Levels by Party Across Survey Waves. Panel A shows mean daily contacts; Panel B shows mask usage (percentage of contacts with masks). Gray background bars indicate weekly COVID-19 cases (in thousands). Democrats (blue) maintained relatively stable behavior while Republicans (red) showed greater variability, particularly in contact behavior."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 11

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Democrat" = "#326ba4", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Republican" = "#c50000")

# Weekly cases data (wave 1: ~200k weekly cases in April 2020)
weekly_cases <- tibble(
  wave = 1:6,
  cases_thousands = c(200, 693, 772, 2351, 2192, 545),
  wave_label = factor(c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"),
                      levels = c("Apr '20", "Jun '20", "Sep '20", "Dec '20", "Feb '21", "May '21"))
)

# Scaling for dual axis
max_cases <- max(weekly_cases$cases_thousands)
max_contacts <- max(behaviors_by_wave$total_contacts)
max_mask <- max(behaviors_by_wave$mask_usage)
contact_scale <- max_contacts / max_cases * 1.3
mask_scale <- max_mask / max_cases * 1.3

# Contacts by party
p_contacts <- ggplot() +
  # Background: cases
  geom_col(data = weekly_cases, aes(x = wave_label, y = cases_thousands * contact_scale),
           fill = "gray80", alpha = 0.5, width = 0.7) +
  # Lines for each party
  geom_line(data = behaviors_by_wave,
            aes(x = wave_label, y = total_contacts, color = political_party, group = political_party),
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(data = behaviors_by_wave,
             aes(x = wave_label, y = total_contacts, color = political_party),
             size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Daily Contacts",
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / contact_scale, name = "Weekly Cases (thousands)"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.1))
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, title = "A. Daily Contacts by Party", color = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "gray50"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Mask usage by party
p_masks <- ggplot() +
  # Background: cases
  geom_col(data = weekly_cases, aes(x = wave_label, y = cases_thousands * mask_scale),
           fill = "gray80", alpha = 0.5, width = 0.7) +
  # Lines for each party
  geom_line(data = behaviors_by_wave,
            aes(x = wave_label, y = mask_usage, color = political_party, group = political_party),
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(data = behaviors_by_wave,
             aes(x = wave_label, y = mask_usage, color = political_party),
             size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Mask Usage (%)",
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / mask_scale, name = "Weekly Cases (thousands)"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.1))
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, title = "B. Mask Usage by Party", color = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "gray50"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Combine with shared legend
p_contacts + p_masks + plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

For contact behavior (Panel A), Democrats maintained a relatively narrow range of daily contacts across all six waves, while Republicans exhibited pronounced swings---increasing contacts substantially during periods of lower case counts (September 2020) and reducing them during surges (December 2020, February 2021). The coefficient of variation for Republican contacts (`r sprintf("%.1f", rep_cv_contacts)`%) was notably higher than for Democrats (`r sprintf("%.1f", dem_cv_contacts)`%), confirming greater Republican variability.

Mask usage (Panel B) shows a different pattern. All parties increased mask-wearing from the early pandemic through winter 2020--21, but Republicans started from a lower baseline and showed steeper declines as vaccines became available in spring 2021. Democrats maintained consistently high mask usage throughout, while Republican mask-wearing tracked more closely with pandemic conditions. This asymmetry---stable Democratic behavior paired with variable Republican behavior---is the primary driver of the fluctuating partisan gaps documented in the main text.

### Formal Test of Variance Differences

```{r}
#| label: levene-test
#| include: false

library(car)

# Load the main dataset for individual-level variance test
bics_data <- read_csv("../data/BICS_ZIP_Features.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  filter(!is.na(political_party) & political_party != "Prefer not to answer")

# Levene's test for equality of variances
levene_result <- leveneTest(num_cc ~ political_party, data = bics_data)

levene_f <- levene_result$`F value`[1]
levene_df1 <- levene_result$Df[1]
levene_df2 <- levene_result$Df[2]
levene_p <- levene_result$`Pr(>F)`[1]

# Calculate individual-level variance by party
variance_by_party <- bics_data %>%
  group_by(political_party) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_contacts = mean(num_cc, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_contacts = sd(num_cc, na.rm = TRUE),
    var_contacts = var(num_cc, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

rep_var <- variance_by_party %>% filter(political_party == "Republican") %>% pull(var_contacts)
dem_var <- variance_by_party %>% filter(political_party == "Democrat") %>% pull(var_contacts)
ind_var <- variance_by_party %>% filter(political_party == "Independent") %>% pull(var_contacts)
```

To formally test whether variance in contact behavior differs across partisan groups, we conducted Levene's test for equality of variances on individual-level contact data. The results confirm significant heterogeneity in variance across parties (F(`r levene_df1`, `r format(levene_df2, big.mark = ",")`) = `r sprintf("%.2f", levene_f)`, p < 0.001). Republicans exhibited the highest variance in daily contacts (`r sprintf("%.1f", rep_var)`), followed by Independents (`r sprintf("%.1f", ind_var)`) and Democrats (`r sprintf("%.1f", dem_var)`). @tbl-variance-test summarizes these results.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-variance-test
#| tbl-cap: "Variance in Daily Contacts by Political Party with Levene's Test"

# Create summary table
variance_table <- variance_by_party %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat"))
  ) %>%
  arrange(political_party) %>%
  select(political_party, n, mean_contacts, sd_contacts, var_contacts)

# Add test results row
test_row <- tibble(
  political_party = "Levene's Test",
  n = NA,
  mean_contacts = NA,
  sd_contacts = sprintf("F = %.2f", levene_f),
  var_contacts = sprintf("p < 0.001")
)

# Combine
final_table <- bind_rows(
  variance_table %>% mutate(across(c(n, mean_contacts, sd_contacts, var_contacts), as.character)),
  test_row
)

kable(final_table,
      col.names = c("Party", "N", "Mean Contacts", "SD", "Variance"),
      digits = 2,
      align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
      booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position")) %>%
  footnote(
    general = "Levene's test assesses equality of variances across groups. Significant result (p < 0.001) indicates variances differ significantly by party.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE
  )
```

The significantly higher variance among Republicans indicates that Republican contact behavior was more responsive to changing conditions---whether pandemic severity, policy environment, or media messaging---while Democrats maintained more consistent behavior throughout the study period.

## Detailed Pandemic Response by Party {#sec-pandemic-response-detail}

@fig-pandemic-response-detail shows the standardized regression coefficients for the relationship between prior week's pandemic conditions (incidence and mortality) and health behaviors (contacts and mask usage), by party. Effects are standardized: bars show the change associated with a one standard deviation increase in log incidence or log mortality.

```{r}
#| label: appendix-setup-response
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Load paper statistics
stats_path <- here::here("out", "tables", "paper_statistics.rds")
paper_stats <- readRDS(stats_path)
invisible(list2env(paper_stats, envir = environment()))

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Democrat" = "#326ba4", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Republican" = "#c50000")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-pandemic-response-detail
#| fig-cap: "Detailed Behavioral Response to Prior Week's Pandemic Conditions by Party. Left column shows changes in daily contacts; right column shows changes in mask usage (percentage points). Top row shows response to incidence; bottom row shows response to mortality. Effects are standardized: bars show the change associated with a one standard deviation increase in log incidence or log mortality. Models include county fixed effects. Error bars indicate standard errors. Negative values for contacts indicate reductions; positive values for masks indicate increases."
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 11

# Create data frame for plotting with standard errors
response_data <- tibble(
  party = rep(c("Democrat", "Independent", "Republican"), 4),
  behavior = rep(c("Contacts", "Contacts", "Masks", "Masks"), each = 3),
  condition = rep(c("Incidence", "Mortality", "Incidence", "Mortality"), each = 3),
  effect = c(
    contacts_inc_effect_dem, contacts_inc_effect_ind, contacts_inc_effect_rep,
    contacts_mort_effect_dem, contacts_mort_effect_ind, contacts_mort_effect_rep,
    masks_inc_effect_dem, masks_inc_effect_ind, masks_inc_effect_rep,
    masks_mort_effect_dem, masks_mort_effect_ind, masks_mort_effect_rep
  ),
  se = c(
    contacts_inc_se_dem, contacts_inc_se_ind, contacts_inc_se_rep,
    contacts_mort_se_dem, contacts_mort_se_ind, contacts_mort_se_rep,
    masks_inc_se_dem, masks_inc_se_ind, masks_inc_se_rep,
    masks_mort_se_dem, masks_mort_se_ind, masks_mort_se_rep
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    party = factor(party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("Incidence", "Mortality"))
  )

# Separate data for contacts and masks
contacts_data <- response_data %>% filter(behavior == "Contacts")
masks_data <- response_data %>% filter(behavior == "Masks")

# Calculate shared y-axis limits for each behavior
contacts_range <- contacts_data %>%
  summarise(ymin = min(effect - se, na.rm = TRUE), ymax = max(effect + se, na.rm = TRUE))
contacts_ylim <- c(min(contacts_range$ymin, -0.1), max(contacts_range$ymax, 0.1)) * 1.15

masks_range <- masks_data %>%
  summarise(ymin = min(effect - se, na.rm = TRUE), ymax = max(effect + se, na.rm = TRUE))
masks_ylim <- c(min(masks_range$ymin, -0.5), max(masks_range$ymax, 0.5)) * 1.15

# Base plot function
make_panel <- function(data, title, y_label, ylimits, show_legend = FALSE) {
  p <- ggplot(data, aes(x = party, y = effect, fill = party)) +
    geom_col(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.7, color = "black") +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = effect - se, ymax = effect + se), width = 0.2, color = "gray30") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    facet_wrap(~ condition, ncol = 1) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = ylimits) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = y_label, title = title) +
    theme_cowplot() +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
      strip.background = element_blank(),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18, hjust = 0.5),
      panel.border = element_rect(color = "gray70", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
    )
  if (!show_legend) p <- p + theme(legend.position = "none")
  return(p)
}

p_contacts <- make_panel(contacts_data, "Daily Contacts", "Change per 1 SD", contacts_ylim)
p_masks <- make_panel(masks_data, "Mask Usage", "Change in pp per 1 SD", masks_ylim)

combined <- p_contacts + p_masks + plot_layout(ncol = 2)
combined + plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

@fig-gap-changes summarizes how partisan gaps change in response to pandemic conditions. This figure complements the individual-level effects shown above by directly displaying the implications for polarization.

```{r}
#| label: fig-gap-changes
#| fig-cap: "Effect of Prior Week's Pandemic Conditions on Partisan Gaps. Bars show how the Republican-Democrat gap changes in the week following a one standard deviation increase in local log incidence or mortality. Negative values indicate gap narrowing (convergence); positive values indicate gap widening (divergence)."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 8

# Calculate how gaps change with pandemic conditions
contacts_gap_change_inc <- contacts_inc_effect_rep - contacts_inc_effect_dem
contacts_gap_change_mort <- contacts_mort_effect_rep - contacts_mort_effect_dem
masks_gap_change_inc <- masks_inc_effect_rep - masks_inc_effect_dem
masks_gap_change_mort <- masks_mort_effect_rep - masks_mort_effect_dem

# Create data for gap changes
gap_change_data <- tibble(
  behavior = c("Contacts", "Contacts", "Masks", "Masks"),
  condition = c("Incidence", "Mortality", "Incidence", "Mortality"),
  gap_change = c(contacts_gap_change_inc, contacts_gap_change_mort,
                 masks_gap_change_inc, masks_gap_change_mort)
) %>%
  mutate(
    label = paste(behavior, "Gap"),
    direction = ifelse(gap_change < 0, "Narrows", "Widens")
  )

ggplot(gap_change_data, aes(x = condition, y = gap_change, fill = direction)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  facet_wrap(~ label, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Narrows" = "#326ba4", "Widens" = "#c50000")) +
  labs(
    x = "Pandemic Condition",
    y = "Change in R-D Gap per 1 SD Increase",
    fill = "Gap"
  ) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.background = element_blank()
  )
```

### Incidence vs. Mortality: Why We Focus on Mortality {#sec-incidence-vs-mortality}

The main text focuses on mortality rather than incidence when discussing partisan responses to pandemic conditions. This section explains that choice and presents the full comparison of incidence and mortality effects.

**Key finding: Mortality was the stronger predictor of contact behavior, while incidence was the stronger predictor of mask-wearing.**

@fig-pandemic-response-detail above displays the full comparison. For contacts (left column), mortality effects are larger than incidence effects for all partisan groups, and statistically significant for Republicans. For masks (right column), the pattern reverses: incidence effects are larger, particularly for Democrats.

This differential pattern has a plausible interpretation:

1. **Mortality and contacts**: Deaths are concrete, visible, and emotionally salient. They are reported in local news, experienced through social networks, and marked by funerals and obituaries. When deaths rise, the threat becomes tangible—prompting reductions in social contact. Republicans showed the largest mortality response, consistent with the main text's finding that their contact behavior swung more dramatically with conditions.

2. **Incidence and masks**: Case counts, by contrast, are more abstract and mediated by testing availability and reporting. However, rising case counts may trigger mask mandates, workplace policies, and social pressure to mask—particularly in areas where cases are visibly surging. Democrats, who were more likely to live in areas with mask mandates and to follow public health guidance, showed the largest incidence response for masks.

@fig-gap-changes summarizes the implications for polarization: mortality narrows the contact gap (Republicans reduce contacts more), while incidence slightly widens the mask gap (Democrats increase masking more). This supports the main text's focus on mortality for the contact behavior analysis, where the key finding is that objective threat can reduce polarization—at least temporarily.

The sections below provide additional detail on mask responses to incidence (@sec-mask-incidence-response) and perception responses to both conditions (@sec-perception-response).

### Mask Usage Response to Incidence {#sec-mask-incidence-response}

@fig-incidence-mask-response parallels @fig-mortality-response in the main text but shows mask usage response to incidence rather than contacts response to mortality. This figure illustrates the finding that incidence was the stronger predictor of mask-wearing, particularly for Democrats.

```{r}
#| label: fig-incidence-mask-response
#| fig-cap: "Mask Usage Response to Incidence. Panel A shows predicted mask usage (percentage of contacts with masks) by party at baseline (average incidence) and when incidence is one standard deviation higher. Panel B shows the Democrat-Republican gap in mask usage at baseline and at higher incidence. All partisan groups increase mask usage when incidence rises, with Democrats showing the largest response."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10

library(patchwork)

# Calculate predicted mask usage at baseline and +1 SD incidence
# Baseline = raw weighted means; +1 SD = baseline + incidence coefficient
pred_masks <- tibble(
  party = rep(c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat"), 2),
  condition = rep(c("Baseline", "+1 SD Incidence"), each = 3),
  mask_usage = c(
    # Baseline (raw means)
    mask_rep, mask_ind, mask_dem,
    # +1 SD incidence (baseline + effect)
    mask_rep + masks_inc_effect_rep,
    mask_ind + masks_inc_effect_ind,
    mask_dem + masks_inc_effect_dem
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    party = factor(party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("Baseline", "+1 SD Incidence"))
  )

# Calculate gaps (D-R since Democrats mask more)
gap_baseline <- mask_dem - mask_rep
gap_high_inc <- (mask_dem + masks_inc_effect_dem) - (mask_rep + masks_inc_effect_rep)

gap_data <- tibble(
  condition = factor(c("Baseline", "+1 SD Incidence"), levels = c("Baseline", "+1 SD Incidence")),
  gap = c(gap_baseline, gap_high_inc)
)

# Panel A: Predicted mask usage by party
p_masks <- ggplot(pred_masks, aes(x = condition, y = mask_usage, fill = party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", mask_usage), group = party),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)", title = "A. Predicted Mask Usage by Party", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Panel B: D-R Gap
p_gap <- ggplot(gap_data, aes(x = condition, y = gap)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, fill = "#1b9e77", alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", gap)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "D-R Gap (pp)", title = "B. Democrat-Republican Gap") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14)
  )

# Combine
p_masks + p_gap + plot_layout(widths = c(1.5, 1))
```

### Perception Response to Pandemic Conditions {#sec-perception-response}

@fig-perception-response shows how COVID-19 concern (the percentage reporting being "very concerned") responded to prior week's pandemic conditions by party. This parallels @fig-pandemic-response-detail but examines perceptions rather than behaviors.

```{r}
#| label: fig-perception-response
#| fig-cap: "Risk Perception Response to Prior Week's Pandemic Conditions by Party. Left column shows response to incidence; right column shows response to mortality. Bars show the change in the percentage reporting being 'very concerned' about COVID-19 associated with a one standard deviation increase in log incidence or log mortality. Models include county fixed effects. Error bars indicate standard errors."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10

library(patchwork)

# Create data frame for perception response plotting
perception_response_data <- tibble(
  party = rep(c("Democrat", "Independent", "Republican"), 2),
  condition = rep(c("Incidence", "Mortality"), each = 3),
  effect = c(
    concern_inc_effect_dem, concern_inc_effect_ind, concern_inc_effect_rep,
    concern_mort_effect_dem, concern_mort_effect_ind, concern_mort_effect_rep
  ),
  se = c(
    concern_inc_se_dem, concern_inc_se_ind, concern_inc_se_rep,
    concern_mort_se_dem, concern_mort_se_ind, concern_mort_se_rep
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    party = factor(party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("Incidence", "Mortality"))
  )

# Calculate y-axis limits
y_range <- perception_response_data %>%
  summarise(ymin = min(effect - se, na.rm = TRUE), ymax = max(effect + se, na.rm = TRUE))
y_lim <- c(min(y_range$ymin, -1), max(y_range$ymax, 1)) * 1.15

# Create the plot
ggplot(perception_response_data, aes(x = party, y = effect, fill = party)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = effect - se, ymax = effect + se), width = 0.2, color = "gray30") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  facet_wrap(~ condition, ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = y_lim) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Change in 'Very Concerned' (pp) per 1 SD") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    strip.background = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "gray70", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  )
```

Unlike health behaviors, which showed clear differential responses to pandemic conditions (mortality predicting contacts, incidence predicting masks), risk perception showed weaker and more inconsistent relationships with local pandemic conditions. This suggests that partisan differences in concern were more stable and less responsive to objective signals about pandemic severity---consistent with the finding in the main text that perception gaps remained relatively constant even as behavior gaps fluctuated.

## Spatial Variation in Partisan Differences {#sec-spatial-variation}

This section provides additional detail on geographic variation in partisan differences. All estimates in this section are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates using regression models, removing potential confounding from differences in when surveys were collected and local pandemic severity. We present behavior levels by party within census regions (complementing the region-level gap maps in the main text), then examine finer geographic scales: census divisions (9 divisions) and states.

@fig-region-levels-map displays adjusted health behavior levels by party and census region.

```{r}
#| label: fig-region-levels-map
#| fig-cap: "Adjusted Health Behavior Levels by Party Across Census Regions. Each row shows a partisan group: Republicans (A-B), Independents (C-D), and Democrats (E-F). Left column shows mean daily contacts; right column shows mask usage. Estimates are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates. Darker shading indicates higher values. Black borders indicate census region boundaries."
#| fig-height: 11
#| fig-width: 10

(p_rep_contacts | p_rep_masks) / (p_ind_contacts | p_ind_masks) / (p_dem_contacts | p_dem_masks)
```

### Urban-Rural Behavior Levels by Party {#sec-metro-detail}

@fig-metro-gaps displays the Republican-Democrat gap in contacts and mask-wearing by metropolitan status. The gaps are smaller in non-metropolitan areas for both behaviors: the contact gap drops from 1.3 to 0.3 additional daily contacts, and the mask gap drops from 9 to 7 percentage points. This pattern helps explain why the South---which has a higher proportion of non-metropolitan residents---showed smaller partisan gaps in the main analysis.

```{r}
#| label: fig-metro-gaps
#| fig-cap: "Republican-Democrat Gaps by Metropolitan Status. Each panel compares the partisan gap in metropolitan versus non-metropolitan areas. Panel A shows the contact gap (R−D) and Panel B shows the mask gap (D−R). Estimates are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 7

library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Load adjusted metro data
behaviors_by_metro <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_behaviors_by_metro.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Calculate R-D gaps by metro status
gaps_by_metro <- behaviors_by_metro %>%
  select(metro_status, political_party, contacts_adj_mean, mask_adj_mean) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = political_party,
              values_from = c(contacts_adj_mean, mask_adj_mean)) %>%
  mutate(
    contacts_gap_rd = contacts_adj_mean_Republican - contacts_adj_mean_Democrat,
    mask_gap_dr = mask_adj_mean_Democrat - mask_adj_mean_Republican,
    metro_status = factor(metro_status, levels = c("Metropolitan", "Non-Metropolitan"))
  )

# Colors for metro status
metro_colors <- c("Metropolitan" = "#4a4a4a", "Non-Metropolitan" = "#a0a0a0")

# Contact gap plot (R-D, so positive = R > D)
p_contact_gap <- ggplot(gaps_by_metro, aes(x = metro_status, y = contacts_gap_rd, fill = metro_status)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", contacts_gap_rd)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = metro_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "R−D Gap (contacts)", title = "A. Contact Gap") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

# Mask gap plot (D-R, so positive = D > R)
p_mask_gap <- ggplot(gaps_by_metro, aes(x = metro_status, y = mask_gap_dr, fill = metro_status)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.0f", mask_gap_dr)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = metro_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "D−R Gap (pp)", title = "B. Mask Gap") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

# Combine horizontally
p_contact_gap + p_mask_gap
```

@fig-metro-behaviors-detail displays health behavior levels by party and metropolitan status, showing that all partisan groups were less cautious in non-metropolitan areas.

```{r}
#| label: fig-metro-behaviors-detail
#| fig-cap: "Adjusted Health Behaviors by Party and Metropolitan Status. Each panel shows behavior levels for the three partisan groups, comparing metropolitan (darker bars) versus non-metropolitan (lighter bars) areas. Panel A shows daily contacts, Panel B shows mask usage, and Panel C shows vaccine acceptance rates (Wave 6 only). Estimates are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates. Error bars indicate standard errors."
#| fig-height: 9
#| fig-width: 6

library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Load adjusted metro data (emmeans with wave + mortality controls)
behaviors_by_metro <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_behaviors_by_metro.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    metro_status = factor(metro_status, levels = c("Metropolitan", "Non-Metropolitan")),
    contacts_mean = contacts_adj_mean,
    contacts_se = contacts_adj_se,
    mask_mean = mask_adj_mean,
    mask_se = mask_adj_se,
    vax_mean = vax_adj_mean,
    vax_se = vax_adj_se
  )

# Party colors with metro/non-metro shading
party_colors_metro <- c(
  "Republican_Metropolitan" = "#c50000",
  "Republican_Non-Metropolitan" = "#ff6666",
  "Independent_Metropolitan" = "#6db753",
  "Independent_Non-Metropolitan" = "#a8d99a",
  "Democrat_Metropolitan" = "#326ba4",
  "Democrat_Non-Metropolitan" = "#7eb3e0"
)

behaviors_by_metro <- behaviors_by_metro %>%
  mutate(party_metro = paste(political_party, metro_status, sep = "_"))

# Contacts plot
p_contacts <- ggplot(behaviors_by_metro, aes(x = political_party, y = contacts_mean, fill = party_metro)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = contacts_mean - contacts_se, ymax = contacts_mean + contacts_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors_metro) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Contacts", title = "A. Contacts", fill = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), legend.position = "none")

# Masks plot
p_masks <- ggplot(behaviors_by_metro, aes(x = political_party, y = mask_mean, fill = party_metro)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mask_mean - mask_se, ymax = mask_mean + mask_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors_metro) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1)), limits = c(0, 80)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)", title = "B. Mask Usage", fill = NULL) +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), legend.position = "none")

# Vaccine acceptance plot
p_vax <- ggplot(behaviors_by_metro, aes(x = political_party, y = vax_mean, fill = party_metro)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = vax_mean - vax_se, ymax = vax_mean + vax_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors_metro,
                    labels = c("Democrat_Metropolitan" = "Metro", "Democrat_Non-Metropolitan" = "Non-Metro",
                              "Independent_Metropolitan" = "Metro", "Independent_Non-Metropolitan" = "Non-Metro",
                              "Republican_Metropolitan" = "Metro", "Republican_Non-Metropolitan" = "Non-Metro")) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1)), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Vaccine Acceptance (%)", title = "C. Vaccine Acceptance", fill = "Metro Status") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), legend.position = "bottom") +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(nrow = 1, override.aes = list(fill = c("#326ba4", "#7eb3e0", "#6db753", "#a8d99a", "#c50000", "#ff6666"))))

# Combine vertically
p_contacts / p_masks / p_vax
```

The figure reveals that the urban-rural shift toward less protective behavior is consistent across all three partisan groups. Notably, Independents show the largest urban-rural differences, particularly for mask-wearing and vaccine acceptance.

### Partisan Gaps Within Policy Environments {#sec-policy-behaviors}

A potential confounder for geographic patterns in partisan gaps is that metropolitan areas were more likely to have strict mask mandates. @fig-policy-behaviors shows that partisan gaps persist within each policy environment, indicating that individual partisanship shapes behavior beyond policy effects.

```{r}
#| label: fig-policy-behaviors
#| fig-cap: "Health Behaviors by Party and Policy Environment. Left column shows behavior by county mask mandate policy (no mandate, less strict, strict). Right column shows behavior by respondent-reported stay-in-place order status (waves 1-5 only; this question was not assessed in wave 6). Top row shows daily contacts; bottom row shows mask usage. Partisan gaps persist within each policy environment."
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 10

library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Load policy behavior data for mask mandates
behaviors_by_policy <- read_csv("../out/tables/behaviors_by_policy.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    policy = factor(policy, levels = c("No Mandate", "Less Strict", "Strict"))
  )

# Load raw data to compute stay-in-place behaviors
bics <- read_csv("../data/BICS_ZIP_Features.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Compute behaviors by stay-in-place policy (waves 1-5 only, wave 6 has all NA)
behaviors_by_sip <- bics %>%
  filter(!is.na(policy_sip), policy_sip %in% c("Yes", "No"),
         political_party %in% c("Democrat", "Independent", "Republican")) %>%
  group_by(policy_sip, political_party) %>%
  summarise(
    contacts_mean = weighted.mean(num_cc, weight_pooled, na.rm = TRUE),
    contacts_se = sqrt(Hmisc::wtd.var(num_cc, weight_pooled, na.rm = TRUE)) / sqrt(n()),
    mask_mean = weighted.mean(Norm_Masks_Used, weight_pooled, na.rm = TRUE),
    mask_se = sqrt(Hmisc::wtd.var(Norm_Masks_Used, weight_pooled, na.rm = TRUE)) / sqrt(sum(!is.na(Norm_Masks_Used))),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    policy_sip = factor(policy_sip, levels = c("No", "Yes"), labels = c("No SIP Order", "SIP Order"))
  )

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Democrat" = "#326ba4", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Republican" = "#c50000")

# A. Contacts by mask mandate
p_contacts_mandate <- ggplot(behaviors_by_policy, aes(x = policy, y = contacts_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = contacts_mean - contacts_se, ymax = contacts_mean + contacts_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Contacts", title = "A. Contacts by Mask Mandate", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9)
  )

# B. Contacts by stay-in-place
p_contacts_sip <- ggplot(behaviors_by_sip, aes(x = policy_sip, y = contacts_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = contacts_mean - contacts_se, ymax = contacts_mean + contacts_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "B. Contacts by Stay-in-Place Order", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9)
  )

# C. Masks by mask mandate
p_masks_mandate <- ggplot(behaviors_by_policy, aes(x = policy, y = mask_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mask_mean - mask_se, ymax = mask_mean + mask_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1)), limits = c(0, 85)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)", title = "C. Mask Usage by Mask Mandate", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9)
  )

# D. Masks by stay-in-place
p_masks_sip <- ggplot(behaviors_by_sip, aes(x = policy_sip, y = mask_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mask_mean - mask_se, ymax = mask_mean + mask_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1)), limits = c(0, 85)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "D. Mask Usage by Stay-in-Place Order", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9)
  )

# Combine in 2x2 grid: rows = behavior, columns = policy type
(p_contacts_mandate + p_contacts_sip) / (p_masks_mandate + p_masks_sip) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Behavior by Local Partisan Context {#sec-partisan-context}

@fig-cd-contacts shows daily contacts by party and local partisan context (congressional district representation). The figure reveals that partisan gaps in contacts persist regardless of local context, though the magnitude varies.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cd-contacts
#| fig-cap: "Daily Contacts by Party and Local Partisan Context. Bars show mean daily contacts for Democrats and Republicans depending on whether they live in a Democratic-led or Republican-led congressional district. Estimates are adjusted for survey wave, local incidence rates, and metropolitan status. Error bars indicate standard errors."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 5

library(cowplot)
library(emmeans)

# Load data
bics <- read_csv("../data/BICS_ZIP_Features.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Filter to main 4 groups
cd_levels <- c("Democrat in Democratic led CD",
               "Democrat in Republican led CD",
               "Republican in Democratic led CD",
               "Republican in Republican led CD")

filtered_df <- bics %>%
  filter(political_party_to_CD %in% cd_levels) %>%
  filter(!is.na(num_cc), !is.na(log_prev_week_inc_rate), !is.na(Metro), !is.na(wave)) %>%
  mutate(
    wave = factor(wave),
    political_party_to_CD = factor(political_party_to_CD, levels = cd_levels)
  )

# Model for contacts
contact_model <- lm(num_cc ~ log_prev_week_inc_rate + Metro + wave + political_party_to_CD,
                    data = filtered_df,
                    weights = weight_pooled)
emm_contact <- as.data.frame(emmeans(contact_model, specs = "political_party_to_CD"))

# Parse the CD context into party and district
emm_contact <- emm_contact %>%
  mutate(
    party = ifelse(grepl("^Democrat", political_party_to_CD), "Democrat", "Republican"),
    district = ifelse(grepl("Democratic led", political_party_to_CD), "Democratic District", "Republican District"),
    party = factor(party, levels = c("Democrat", "Republican")),
    district = factor(district, levels = c("Democratic District", "Republican District"))
  )

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Democrat" = "#326ba4", "Republican" = "#c50000")

# Contacts plot
ggplot(emm_contact, aes(x = district, y = emmean, fill = party, group = party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = emmean - SE, ymax = emmean + SE),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Contacts", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10)
  )
```

@fig-cd-gaps shows the Republican-Democrat gap in each behavior by local partisan context. For contacts, the R-D gap is larger in Democratic-led districts than in Republican-led districts, suggesting that Republicans increase contacts more than Democrats when living in Democratic areas. For masks, the D-R gap is similar across contexts, indicating that both parties shift their mask behavior in parallel when living in opposing-party areas.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cd-gaps
#| fig-cap: "Partisan Gaps by Local Context. Panel A shows the Republican-Democrat gap in daily contacts; Panel B shows the Democrat-Republican gap in mask usage. Gaps are calculated within each congressional district context (Democratic-led vs Republican-led). Estimates are adjusted for survey wave, local incidence rates, and metropolitan status."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 8

library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)
library(emmeans)

# Load data
bics <- read_csv("../data/BICS_ZIP_Features.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Filter to main 4 groups
cd_levels <- c("Democrat in Democratic led CD",
               "Democrat in Republican led CD",
               "Republican in Democratic led CD",
               "Republican in Republican led CD")

filtered_df <- bics %>%
  filter(political_party_to_CD %in% cd_levels) %>%
  filter(!is.na(num_cc), !is.na(log_prev_week_inc_rate), !is.na(Metro), !is.na(wave)) %>%
  mutate(
    wave = factor(wave),
    political_party_to_CD = factor(political_party_to_CD, levels = cd_levels)
  )

# Model for contacts
contact_model <- lm(num_cc ~ log_prev_week_inc_rate + Metro + wave + political_party_to_CD,
                    data = filtered_df,
                    weights = weight_pooled)
emm_contact <- as.data.frame(emmeans(contact_model, specs = "political_party_to_CD"))

# Model for masks
mask_model <- lm(Norm_Masks_Used ~ log_prev_week_inc_rate + Metro + wave + political_party_to_CD,
                 data = filtered_df,
                 weights = weight_pooled)
emm_mask <- as.data.frame(emmeans(mask_model, specs = "political_party_to_CD"))

# Calculate gaps by context
gaps_by_context <- tibble(
  context = c("Democratic\nDistrict", "Republican\nDistrict"),
  contacts_gap_rd = c(
    emm_contact$emmean[emm_contact$political_party_to_CD == "Republican in Democratic led CD"] -
      emm_contact$emmean[emm_contact$political_party_to_CD == "Democrat in Democratic led CD"],
    emm_contact$emmean[emm_contact$political_party_to_CD == "Republican in Republican led CD"] -
      emm_contact$emmean[emm_contact$political_party_to_CD == "Democrat in Republican led CD"]
  ),
  masks_gap_dr = c(
    emm_mask$emmean[emm_mask$political_party_to_CD == "Democrat in Democratic led CD"] -
      emm_mask$emmean[emm_mask$political_party_to_CD == "Republican in Democratic led CD"],
    emm_mask$emmean[emm_mask$political_party_to_CD == "Democrat in Republican led CD"] -
      emm_mask$emmean[emm_mask$political_party_to_CD == "Republican in Republican led CD"]
  )
) %>%
  mutate(context = factor(context, levels = c("Democratic\nDistrict", "Republican\nDistrict")))

# Context colors
context_colors <- c("Democratic\nDistrict" = "#326ba4", "Republican\nDistrict" = "#c50000")

# Contacts gap plot
p_contacts_gap <- ggplot(gaps_by_context, aes(x = context, y = contacts_gap_rd, fill = context)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", contacts_gap_rd)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = context_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "R−D Gap (contacts)", title = "A. Contact Gap") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

# Masks gap plot
p_masks_gap <- ggplot(gaps_by_context, aes(x = context, y = masks_gap_dr, fill = context)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.0f", masks_gap_dr)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = context_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "D−R Gap (pp)", title = "B. Mask Gap") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

# Combine
p_contacts_gap + p_masks_gap
```

### Regional Behavior Levels

@fig-behaviors-by-region displays the raw behavior levels (mean contacts and mask usage) for each party within each census region. This complements the gap-focused maps in the main text by showing the underlying distributions.

```{r}
#| label: load-region-data-appendix
#| include: false

# Load regional data - using adjusted estimates (emmeans with wave + mortality controls)
behaviors_by_region <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_behaviors_by_region.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    census_region = factor(census_region, levels = c("Northeast", "Midwest", "South", "West"))
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-behaviors-by-region
#| fig-cap: "Adjusted Health Behaviors by Party Across Census Regions. Panel A shows mean daily contacts; Panel B shows mask usage (percentage of contacts with masks). Estimates are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates. Error bars indicate standard errors from emmeans."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 11

library(cowplot)
library(patchwork)

# Party colors
party_colors <- c("Republican" = "#c50000", "Independent" = "#6db753", "Democrat" = "#326ba4")

# Contacts by region
p_contacts <- ggplot(behaviors_by_region, aes(x = census_region, y = contacts_adj_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = contacts_adj_mean - contacts_adj_se, ymax = contacts_adj_mean + contacts_adj_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Contacts", title = "A. Daily Contacts by Region", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Masks by region
p_masks <- ggplot(behaviors_by_region, aes(x = census_region, y = mask_adj_mean, fill = political_party)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mask_adj_mean - mask_adj_se, ymax = mask_adj_mean + mask_adj_se),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1)), limits = c(0, 80)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mask Usage (%)", title = "B. Mask Usage by Region", fill = "Party") +
  theme_cowplot() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Combine
p_contacts + p_masks + plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The figure reveals that the smaller partisan gaps in the South are driven primarily by Republicans in that region reporting fewer contacts and higher mask usage than Republicans elsewhere, rather than by Democrats in the South behaving differently. This suggests that regional norms may exert a moderating influence on Republican behavior specifically.


### Regional Vaccine Acceptance and Perception Maps by Party

@fig-region-vax-perception-map displays vaccine acceptance rates (Wave 6 only) and pandemic concern levels by party across census regions. These complement the contact and mask usage maps above.

```{r}
#| label: fig-region-vax-perception-map
#| fig-cap: "Adjusted Vaccine Acceptance and Perception Levels by Party Across Census Regions. Top row shows vaccine acceptance rates (Wave 6, May 2021) for Democrats (A) and Republicans (B). Bottom row shows percentage reporting being 'very concerned' about COVID-19 for Democrats (C) and Republicans (D). Estimates are adjusted for local mortality rates. Darker shading indicates higher values. Black borders indicate census region boundaries."
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 10

library(usmap)
library(patchwork)
library(sf)

# Load adjusted vaccination and perception data
adj_vax_by_region <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_vax_by_region.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    census_region = factor(census_region, levels = c("Northeast", "Midwest", "South", "West"))
  )

adj_perception_by_region <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_perception_by_region.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    political_party = factor(political_party, levels = c("Republican", "Independent", "Democrat")),
    census_region = factor(census_region, levels = c("Northeast", "Midwest", "South", "West"))
  )

# Create region-to-state mapping
region_map <- tibble(
  state = c(
    "Connecticut", "Maine", "Massachusetts", "New Hampshire", "New Jersey",
    "New York", "Pennsylvania", "Rhode Island", "Vermont",
    "Illinois", "Indiana", "Iowa", "Kansas", "Michigan", "Minnesota",
    "Missouri", "Nebraska", "North Dakota", "Ohio", "South Dakota", "Wisconsin",
    "Alabama", "Arkansas", "Delaware", "District of Columbia", "Florida",
    "Georgia", "Kentucky", "Louisiana", "Maryland", "Mississippi",
    "North Carolina", "Oklahoma", "South Carolina", "Tennessee", "Texas",
    "Virginia", "West Virginia",
    "Alaska", "Arizona", "California", "Colorado", "Hawaii", "Idaho",
    "Montana", "Nevada", "New Mexico", "Oregon", "Utah", "Washington", "Wyoming"
  ),
  census_region = c(
    rep("Northeast", 9), rep("Midwest", 12), rep("South", 17), rep("West", 13)
  )
)

# Reshape data for mapping
dem_vax <- adj_vax_by_region %>%
  filter(political_party == "Democrat") %>%
  select(census_region, vax_adj_mean)

rep_vax <- adj_vax_by_region %>%
  filter(political_party == "Republican") %>%
  select(census_region, vax_adj_mean)

dem_concern <- adj_perception_by_region %>%
  filter(political_party == "Democrat") %>%
  select(census_region, concern_adj_mean)

rep_concern <- adj_perception_by_region %>%
  filter(political_party == "Republican") %>%
  select(census_region, concern_adj_mean)

dem_vax_map <- region_map %>% left_join(dem_vax, by = "census_region")
rep_vax_map <- region_map %>% left_join(rep_vax, by = "census_region")
dem_concern_map <- region_map %>% left_join(dem_concern, by = "census_region")
rep_concern_map <- region_map %>% left_join(rep_concern, by = "census_region")

# Get region boundaries
us_states_sf <- us_map(regions = "states") %>%
  left_join(region_map, by = c("full" = "state"))

region_boundaries <- us_states_sf %>%
  group_by(census_region) %>%
  summarise(geometry = st_union(geom), .groups = "drop")

# Set common scales
vax_range <- range(c(dem_vax$vax_adj_mean, rep_vax$vax_adj_mean), na.rm = TRUE)
concern_range <- range(c(dem_concern$concern_adj_mean, rep_concern$concern_adj_mean), na.rm = TRUE)

# Democrat vaccine acceptance
p_dem_vax <- plot_usmap(data = dem_vax_map, values = "vax_adj_mean", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#deebf7", high = "#2171b5", name = "Vax %", limits = vax_range, na.value = "gray90") +
  labs(title = "A. Democrat Vaccine Acceptance") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, hjust = 0.5), legend.position = "right")

# Republican vaccine acceptance
p_rep_vax <- plot_usmap(data = rep_vax_map, values = "vax_adj_mean", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#fee0d2", high = "#cb181d", name = "Vax %", limits = vax_range, na.value = "gray90") +
  labs(title = "B. Republican Vaccine Acceptance") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, hjust = 0.5), legend.position = "right")

# Democrat concern
p_dem_concern <- plot_usmap(data = dem_concern_map, values = "concern_adj_mean", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#deebf7", high = "#2171b5", name = "Concern %", limits = concern_range, na.value = "gray90") +
  labs(title = "C. Democrat Concern") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, hjust = 0.5), legend.position = "right")

# Republican concern
p_rep_concern <- plot_usmap(data = rep_concern_map, values = "concern_adj_mean", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#fee0d2", high = "#cb181d", name = "Concern %", limits = concern_range, na.value = "gray90") +
  labs(title = "D. Republican Concern") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, hjust = 0.5), legend.position = "right")

# Combine in 2x2 grid
(p_dem_vax | p_rep_vax) / (p_dem_concern | p_rep_concern)
```

Vaccine acceptance rates show strong partisan gaps across all regions, with Democrats consistently reporting higher acceptance rates than Republicans. The gap is largest in the South and Midwest. Perception of pandemic severity (concern) shows an even starker partisan divide: Democrats report being "very concerned" about COVID-19 at rates 20-30 percentage points higher than Republicans in every region. Interestingly, while Republican concern varies less across regions, Democratic concern is highest in the Northeast and West.

### All Partisan Gaps by Census Region

@fig-region-all-gaps displays partisan gaps across all four outcomes in a single 2×2 view, facilitating comparison of geographic patterns across behaviors and perceptions.

```{r}
#| label: fig-region-all-gaps
#| fig-cap: "Geographic Distribution of All Partisan Gaps by Census Region (Adjusted). Panel A shows the Republican-Democrat gap in daily contacts (purple); Panel B shows the Democrat-Republican gap in mask usage (gold); Panel C shows the Democrat-Republican gap in vaccine acceptance (teal); Panel D shows the Democrat-Republican gap in COVID-19 concern (orange). Estimates are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates. Black borders indicate census region boundaries."
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 10

library(usmap)
library(patchwork)
library(sf)

# Create region-to-state mapping
region_map <- tibble(
  state = c(
    "Connecticut", "Maine", "Massachusetts", "New Hampshire", "New Jersey",
    "New York", "Pennsylvania", "Rhode Island", "Vermont",
    "Illinois", "Indiana", "Iowa", "Kansas", "Michigan", "Minnesota",
    "Missouri", "Nebraska", "North Dakota", "Ohio", "South Dakota", "Wisconsin",
    "Alabama", "Arkansas", "Delaware", "District of Columbia", "Florida",
    "Georgia", "Kentucky", "Louisiana", "Maryland", "Mississippi",
    "North Carolina", "Oklahoma", "South Carolina", "Tennessee", "Texas",
    "Virginia", "West Virginia",
    "Alaska", "Arizona", "California", "Colorado", "Hawaii", "Idaho",
    "Montana", "Nevada", "New Mexico", "Oregon", "Utah", "Washington", "Wyoming"
  ),
  census_region = c(
    rep("Northeast", 9), rep("Midwest", 12), rep("South", 17), rep("West", 13)
  )
)

# Load all adjusted data
gaps_by_region <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_gaps_by_region.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
adj_vax_by_region <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_vax_by_region.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
adj_perception_by_region <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_perception_by_region.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Calculate vaccination gaps (D-R)
vax_gaps <- adj_vax_by_region %>%
  select(census_region, political_party, vax_adj_mean) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = political_party, values_from = vax_adj_mean) %>%
  mutate(vax_gap_dr = Democrat - Republican) %>%
  select(census_region, vax_gap_dr)

# Calculate perception gaps (D-R)
perception_gaps <- adj_perception_by_region %>%
  select(census_region, political_party, concern_adj_mean) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = political_party, values_from = concern_adj_mean) %>%
  mutate(concern_gap_dr = Democrat - Republican) %>%
  select(census_region, concern_gap_dr)

# Merge all gaps with region map
region_gaps_for_map <- region_map %>%
  left_join(gaps_by_region, by = "census_region") %>%
  left_join(vax_gaps, by = "census_region") %>%
  left_join(perception_gaps, by = "census_region")

# Get state polygons and create region boundaries
us_states_sf <- us_map(regions = "states") %>%
  left_join(region_map, by = c("full" = "state"))

region_boundaries <- us_states_sf %>%
  group_by(census_region) %>%
  summarise(geometry = st_union(geom), .groups = "drop")

# Contact gap map - purple gradient (R > D)
p_contact_map <- plot_usmap(data = region_gaps_for_map, values = "contacts_gap_rd", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#f2e6ff", high = "#5c2d91",
    name = "R−D\n(contacts)",
    na.value = "gray90"
  ) +
  labs(title = "A. Contact Gap") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

# Mask gap map - gold gradient (D > R)
p_mask_map <- plot_usmap(data = region_gaps_for_map, values = "mask_gap_dr", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#fff7e6", high = "#b8860b",
    name = "D−R\n(pp)",
    na.value = "gray90"
  ) +
  labs(title = "B. Mask Gap") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

# Vaccine acceptance gap map - teal gradient (D > R)
p_vax_map <- plot_usmap(data = region_gaps_for_map, values = "vax_gap_dr", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
```
