```{r}
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#e0f4f4", high = "#006d6d",
    name = "D−R\n(pp)",
    na.value = "gray90"
  ) +
  labs(title = "C. Vaccine Acceptance Gap") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

# Perception gap map - orange gradient (D > R)
p_concern_map <- plot_usmap(data = region_gaps_for_map, values = "concern_gap_dr", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#fff3e6", high = "#d45500",
    name = "D−R\n(pp)",
    na.value = "gray90"
  ) +
  labs(title = "D. Concern Gap") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

# Arrange in 2x2 grid
(p_contact_map | p_mask_map) / (p_vax_map | p_concern_map)
```

The 2×2 view reveals that geographic patterns differ across outcomes. Contact gaps (A) and mask gaps (B) show the most regional variation, with the South having notably smaller gaps. In contrast, vaccine acceptance gaps (C) and especially concern gaps (D) are more uniform across regions, suggesting that these outcomes are more strongly driven by partisanship itself rather than regional context.

### Partisan Gaps by Census Division {#sec-division-maps}

@fig-division-gap-map displays partisan gaps at the census division level (9 divisions), providing more geographic granularity than the 4 census regions. **Note**: The East South Central division (AL, KY, MS, TN) shows a reversed pattern with Democrats reporting more contacts and higher mask usage than Republicans. This anomaly likely reflects sampling variability given the smaller sample sizes in that division (n=166 Democrats, n=205 Republicans).

```{r}
#| label: fig-division-gap-map
#| fig-cap: "Geographic Distribution of Adjusted Partisan Gaps by Census Division. Panel A shows the Republican-Democrat gap in daily contacts (purple = Republicans report more contacts; teal = Democrats report more). Panel B shows the Democrat-Republican gap in mask usage (gold = Democrats mask more; teal = Republicans mask more). Estimates are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates. White indicates no gap. Black borders indicate census division boundaries."
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 8

library(usmap)
library(patchwork)
library(sf)

# Load division gaps - using adjusted estimates
gaps_by_division <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_gaps_by_division.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Create division-to-state mapping
division_map <- tibble(
  state = c(
    # New England
    "Connecticut", "Maine", "Massachusetts", "New Hampshire", "Rhode Island", "Vermont",
    # Middle Atlantic
    "New Jersey", "New York", "Pennsylvania",
    # East North Central
    "Illinois", "Indiana", "Michigan", "Ohio", "Wisconsin",
    # West North Central
    "Iowa", "Kansas", "Minnesota", "Missouri", "Nebraska", "North Dakota", "South Dakota",
    # South Atlantic
    "Delaware", "District of Columbia", "Florida", "Georgia", "Maryland",
    "North Carolina", "South Carolina", "Virginia", "West Virginia",
    # East South Central
    "Alabama", "Kentucky", "Mississippi", "Tennessee",
    # West South Central
    "Arkansas", "Louisiana", "Oklahoma", "Texas",
    # Mountain
    "Arizona", "Colorado", "Idaho", "Montana", "Nevada", "New Mexico", "Utah", "Wyoming",
    # Pacific
    "Alaska", "California", "Hawaii", "Oregon", "Washington"
  ),
  census_division = c(
    rep("New England", 6), rep("Middle Atlantic", 3),
    rep("East North Central", 5), rep("West North Central", 7),
    rep("South Atlantic", 9), rep("East South Central", 4), rep("West South Central", 4),
    rep("Mountain", 8), rep("Pacific", 5)
  )
)

# Merge with gaps data
division_gaps_for_map <- division_map %>%
  left_join(gaps_by_division, by = "census_division")

# Get state polygons and create division boundaries
us_states_sf <- us_map(regions = "states") %>%
  left_join(division_map, by = c("full" = "state"))

division_boundaries <- us_states_sf %>%
  group_by(census_division) %>%
  summarise(geometry = st_union(geom), .groups = "drop")

# Contact gap map - diverging scale: purple (R>D) vs teal (D>R)
p_contact_map <- plot_usmap(data = division_gaps_for_map, values = "contacts_gap_rd", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = division_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#1b9e77", mid = "white", high = "#5c2d91",
    midpoint = 0,
    name = "R−D Gap\n(contacts)",
    na.value = "gray90"
  ) +
  labs(title = "A. Contact Gap (Republican − Democrat)") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

# Mask gap map - diverging scale: gold (D>R) vs teal (R>D)
p_mask_map <- plot_usmap(data = division_gaps_for_map, values = "mask_gap_dr", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = division_boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#1b9e77", mid = "white", high = "#b8860b",
    midpoint = 0,
    name = "D−R Gap\n(pp)",
    na.value = "gray90"
  ) +
  labs(title = "B. Mask Gap (Democrat − Republican)") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

# Stack vertically
p_contact_map / p_mask_map
```

### State-Level Partisan Gaps {#sec-state-maps}

@fig-state-maps provides a more granular view, mapping state-level partisan differences in contacts and mask usage. **Important caveat**: The BICS sample was designed to be nationally representative, not representative at the state level. State-level estimates should therefore be interpreted with caution, as they may reflect sampling variability rather than true geographic differences. We present these maps for exploratory purposes only; the regional and division-level analyses above provide more reliable geographic comparisons. States with fewer than 10 respondents per party are excluded.

```{r}
#| label: fig-state-maps
#| fig-cap: "State-Level Adjusted Partisan Gaps in Health Behaviors. Panel A shows the Republican-Democrat gap in daily contacts (purple = Republicans report more; teal = Democrats report more). Panel B shows the Democrat-Republican gap in mask usage (gold = Democrats mask more; teal = Republicans mask more). Estimates are adjusted for survey wave and local mortality rates. White indicates no gap; gray states have insufficient sample size."
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 10

library(usmap)
library(patchwork)

# Load state-level gaps - using adjusted estimates
gaps_by_state <- read_csv("../out/tables/adj_gaps_by_state.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(state = STATE)  # usmap needs 'state' column with full names

# Contact gap map - diverging: purple (R>D) vs teal (D>R)
p_contacts_map <- plot_usmap(data = gaps_by_state, values = "contacts_gap_rd", color = "gray50") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#1b9e77", mid = "white", high = "#5c2d91",
    midpoint = 0,
    name = "R−D Gap\n(contacts)",
    na.value = "gray90",
    limits = c(-5, 7)
  ) +
  labs(title = "A. Contact Gap (Republican − Democrat)") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

# Mask gap map - diverging: gold (D>R) vs teal (R>D)
p_masks_map <- plot_usmap(data = gaps_by_state, values = "mask_gap_dr", color = "gray50") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#1b9e77", mid = "white", high = "#b8860b",
    midpoint = 0,
    name = "D−R Gap\n(pp)",
    na.value = "gray90",
    limits = c(-10, 35)
  ) +
  labs(title = "B. Mask Gap (Democrat − Republican)") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

# Combine vertically
p_contacts_map / p_masks_map
```

The maps reveal substantial heterogeneity in partisan gaps across states. For contact behavior, most states show the expected pattern of Republicans reporting more contacts than Democrats (red shading), but the magnitude varies considerably---from nearly no gap in some Southern states to gaps exceeding 5 contacts per day in states like Michigan and Colorado. A few states show reversed patterns (blue shading) where Democrats report more contacts, though these often reflect small sample sizes.

For mask usage, the Democrat-Republican gap (blue shading indicates Democrats masking more) is positive in nearly all states, but again varies substantially in magnitude. Western states tend to show larger mask gaps, while some Southern states show smaller differences---consistent with the regional patterns documented in the main text.
